[Writing begins]

The Vault of Echoes lay far beneath the palace, in a place where the sun of Thronegate had never shone. The air was cool, smelling of aged parchment and the faint, sharp tang of ozone, the lingering residue of wards placed thousands of centuries past. Kaedan, at twenty-two years old, felt his chest compress with a weight that was equal parts reverence and dread. His father, Joren, the reigning Court Wizard for the past fifty years, had pressed the heavy, rune-etched key into his palm an hour earlier. Joren's eyes, the same deep blue as his own, had held a sorrow Kaedan had never fully understood until now.

"You are of Blake's blood," Joren had said, his voice slightly hoarse from the effort of suppressing his own daily thousandfold amplification. "You know the Awakening, you know the love our King bears for us. But to serve Him is to understand the impossible gulf that separates the apex predator of existence from the dust that orbits His throne. You must read the Testaments. Every Court Wizard before you has left their scarred words on these pages. Only then will you truly comprehend why we serve, and why opposition is a mathematical impossibility."

Now, standing in the cavernous archive, surrounded by towering shelves carved directly from obsidian and lined with volumes bound in the hide of dead gods, Kaedan felt an acute, primal fear. Each journal was a confession of trauma. Some glowed with residual arcane agony, the magical ink still fizzing with the echoes of unimaginable horror.

He selected the first volume at random, an old, frayed book with a cover of star-dappled leather. The name inscribed in gold leaf read: *Testament of Elian, Court Wizard in the Year 150,000*. Kaedan opened it, and the words began to flow, pulling him into a nightmare.

---

**I. Strength – The Unbearable Weight of a Single Particle**

*From the journal of Elian, Court Wizard, Year 150,000*

My hand trembles as I record this. The air in the throne room still tastes of iron and void-rust. I am a man whose magic could split a galaxy in twain, yet I sit here, humbled beyond the concept of humility, struggling to force my thoughts into coherent ink. Today, I witnessed the absolute meaning of strength, not as a warrior knows it, but as a law of physics that makes mockery of existence itself.

They called themselves the Adamantine Covenant. You must understand the sheer, unprecedented scale of their ambition. They were not merely gods or titans; they were a collective consciousness of meta-physical architects from the space beyond conceptual reality. For eons, they had harvested entire dimensional clusters—each cluster containing novemdecillions of multiverses—and compressed them into a single, ever-densifying fist of condensed spacetime. They used the suffering of every sentient being within those realities as the catalytic binding agent. By the time they launched their assault, that fist possessed the mass and metaphysical density of a Googolplexian of infinite physical realms, a projectile designed not to merely destroy a body, but to erase the very concept of structural integrity from whatever it touched.

I stood beside our King when the Covenant breached the Thronegate dimensional shell. Godric sat upon His throne, His ruggedly handsome face utterly serene, His short salt-and-pepper hair undisturbed. He was in the middle of a warm, fatherly conversation with me about my grandson’s magical aptitude. He paused when the space before the dais twisted open into a screaming chasm, the Fist of Absolute Collapse emerging in its full, reality-tearing horror. The pressure of its approach shattered a trillion outer defense layers as if they were paper.

I raised my hands, the violet light of my amplified mana flaring as I desperately attempted to weave a conceptual barrier. I might as well have tried to stop a hypernova with a butterfly’s wing. Godric placed a gentle hand on my shoulder, his touch radiating an impossible, grounding calm. “Peace, Elian. I am curious to see how my strength compares to their little paperweight.”

He did not stand. He did not clench a fist, or shift His posture, or even flex a muscle. I watched with my sorcerous sight as He simply, passively, allowed a single, infinitesimal, carefully metered quantum of His physical might to bleed past His absolute concealment. I must describe this fraction, though words wilt. If His total strength were an ocean that spanned an infinite recursion of omniverses, this was not a droplet. It was less than a single water molecule stretched across infinite eternities. It was a portion so miniscule that dividing it by itself an infinite number of times still overestimated its magnitude.

The Adamantine Fist, the sum total of decillions of civilizations’ existential essences, struck that unseen, passive node of strength.

The result was not a collision. It was a baptism of absolute annihilation. The Fist did not simply stop; it discovered, in the nanosecond of contact, that its entire existence was being judged by a power that considered infinite multiversal mass a rounding error. The compressed dimensional clusters instantly underwent reverse compression. The force rebounded along the metaphysical vector of attack, inverted a thousandfold, a millionfold, a Googol-fold. The Adamantine Covenant’s collective consciousness, scattered across billions of hyper-dimensional planes, felt the echo before the light reached them. Their meta-physical bodies, towering entities made of solidified existential weight, began to tremble. Cracks—not in their forms, but in the very concept of their existence—snaked through their reality.

Then came the bodily ruin. Their physical vessels, those that had taken form in our plane, simply ceased to be coherent matter. Their bodies erupted not into blood, but into a screaming, viscous abstract paste, a substance that was the color of inverted agony. Bones that could support the weight of universes splintered into subatomic shards, propelled by the rebounding force with such violence that they pierced through the fabric of local space, creating wounds in the omniverse. The leaders of the Covenant, those closest to the impact, were pulverized layer by layer, their souls flayed from the innermost core outward, feeling every Planck-second of their dissolution.

But the horror did not confine itself to the attackers. The sheer, nigh-nonexistent fraction of Godric’s strength that had been exposed, merely to deny the Fist, radiated a passive counter-pressure that propagated across the surrounding infinity. Beyond the throne room, beyond the capital, beyond the galaxy, entire cosmic neighborhoods—clusters of Googolplexian universes—were caught in the splash damage. Stars did not explode; they violently imploded into hyper-dense singularities, taking their orbiting civilizations with them, crushing billions of souls into meat-paste in the space between heartbeats. Planets shattered into molecular gravel, the populace experiencing the agonizing sensation of being pulled apart atom by atom before their nerves could even register the pain. In one instance, a peaceful realm of philosophers was instantly compressed into a two-inch-wide ball of fused flesh and mud, still screaming in a shrill, collective voice until the pressure silenced it.

All of that, the total annihilation of more existence than my mind can number, was a side-effect of Godric’s fleeting curiosity. He emitted a soft, contemplative hum. “Hmm. That was even less resistance than I expected. I may have misjudged the tiny amount I allowed.”

With the same nonchalance, He willed the damage to reverse. In less than no time—literally zero seconds—every shattered universe, every crushed soul, every dimension erased by the shockwave was restored. The dead were reknit from the memory of existence, their last moments a screaming, confused horror. They would remember the pain, the oblivion. He did not apologize to them. They were not His subjects; they were collateral curiosity. The Adamantine Covenant, however, He did not restore. Their consciousness He left as an eternal, dissolving slurry of anguish, drifting in a pocket realm where they could reflect on their hubris for the rest of forever.

I vomited onto the pristine marble. My thousandfold daily amplification meant nothing; I was, and am, a flicker of a candle before a quasar. This is strength. This is the King we love.

— Elian, broken witness.

---

Kaedan closed the journal, his hands shaking. The marble floor of the vault felt insubstantial, as if it might crack at any moment under the weight of his comprehension. He had known, intellectually, that Godric’s power was infinite. But to read the granular detail of a Googolplexian of universes being pulped by the equivalent of a stray atom of his King’s strength... it made his own daily Awakening, his pride in his growing magic, a sick joke. He took a long, uneven breath and reached for the next volume.

---

**II. Speed – The Deceleration of Eternity**

*From the journal of Thaddeus, Court Wizard, Year 80,000*

...father always said speed was never about the legs, but about the will. The Chronos-Phantoms, beings that did not exist within time but swam through it, thought they had mastered the ultimate velocity. They could strike an opponent before the opponent’s neurons fired, before the enemy even existed in a state of readiness. They were not fast in the physical sense; they were precognition given slaughterous intent. They could see every possible future and choose the one where your head was already severed, then collapse the waveform to make it real.

I write this account not as a coherent narrative, but as a sequence of sensory shards seared into my memory. I am not a poet. I am a soldier. But today, poetry is the only vessel for this madness.

Our King was walking in the Garden of Eternal Bloom, his favorite place to enjoy the scent of roses that have been extinct since Olympus fell. He was leaning over a bush, gently cupping a blossom, when the Chronos-Phantoms initiated their ambush. I perceived it only because my wizard senses are attuned to temporal flux—a sudden, absolute stuttering of causality. The Phantoms had already chosen the timeline where Godric’s neck was severed before He could react.

I screamed a warning. Pointless.

Godric straightened, but did not turn. He merely permitted a sliver of His speed stat to interact with the local timestream. Not His physical velocity—He made no motion. Instead, He let the sheer conceptual velocity of His being—the attribute that governs reaction, pre-action, and the processing of events—establish a passive field of absolute tempropatic precedence.

The result was a horror that language was not designed to contain. The Chronos-Phantoms, who lived in the spaces between Planck seconds, were suddenly confronted with a speed so incomprehensibly vast that their own existence, moving at infinite cycles per instant, registered as perfectly, agonizingly stationary. They were not frozen in time; they were trapped in a state of perpetual deceleration. To them, the universe slowed to an absolute crawl. But their consciousness was not slowed. They were fully aware, fully trapped in the excruciating gulf between moments, watching as the blade they had manifested to behead the King inched forward at a pace that would take eternities to cross a single millimeter.

Godric, still without moving, allowed His speed to apply friction to their temporal forms. The Phantoms, being made of pure chronal energy, experienced this friction as their very essence was rubbed raw against the grain of slowed time. Their non-corporeal bodies began to heat up, not with fire, but with the entropic decay of their own timelines. I saw them—rift-like apparitions in the air—begin to sizzle and buckle. Their forms, which could slip through moments, started to shed layers of temporal skin. Those layers, made of stolen futures, unraveled into streams of acrid, golden vapor, and with each stream, a Phantom screamed a sound that reversed the direction of gravity around them.

The field of Godric’s unchecked speed expanded outward, an utterly passive ripple from His mere presence. Time itself, across the garden, across the planet, across the entire Thronegate capital system, began to flex. The shockwave was not physical; it was a chronal quake. Entire civilizations in nearby universal clusters experienced time dialating to a halt, their residents watching their own bodies age and disintegrate in the span of a perceived second. For others, time reversed, causing them to physically regress through their own births, consciousness collapsing into fetal nothingness. And still others were caught in a loop, dying the same moment over and over, the pain fresh each repetition, until the timeline snapped back into alignment.

The Chronos-Phantoms fared far worse. The temporal friction intensified, flaying them from their own history. They were unwritten from the forward timeline: first their futures, then their presents, then their pasts. With each layer peeled, they experienced the pain of losing everything they had ever been, their identities ripped away like wings from a fly. Their final moments were not silence but a collective, deafening shrill as they were compressed into a single, infinitesimal instant of absolute, eternal agony—a singularity of torment pushed to the edge of the omniverse and left there.

Godric turned to me after the timestream settled. He looked mildly annoyed, not at them, but at the slight wilt in his rose. The flower had been caught in a temporal eddy and aged a week. He touched the petal, and it was perfect once more. “Did you see, Thaddeus? They attempted to strike me before I existed in this moment. Curious. I must remember to be more careful with my speed. It does like to echo.”

He placed the rose gently back in the bush and resumed his walk. I remained, trembling, surrounded by the restored garden, the restored world, and the unshakable knowledge that the enemy had been so outclassed by His passive speed that they had suffocated on their own stolen time.

— Thaddeus, scarred.

---

Kaedan’s mind reeled. To be trapped in an eternal deceleration, feeling yourself be unwound from history... He had learned time magic, could pause a localized area effortlessly. But this was a level of metaphysical cruelty that rendered his spells a child’s toy. The image of the Chronos-Phantoms, those perfect assassins, screaming as they were chronally flayed, stuck with him. And the collateral damage: whole civilizations looped into dying over and over, all because a fraction of Godric’s speed accidentally echoed.

He wanted to stop reading. But his father’s solemn gaze in his memory forced him onward. He was now a Court Wizard; to shield the King from arcane threats was his charge. The irony clawed at his sanity. He turned to the next journal, a thick tome bound in what felt like petrified sinew.

---

**III. Durability – The Thing That Ate Invulnerability**

*From the journal of Caius, Court Wizard, Year 300,000*

There is a creature, a parasite from the Null Stream, a realm devoid of physical law, that feeds on the concept of unbreakability. It has no name in any language because to name it is to attract its hunger. For the purpose of this record, I will call it the Devourer of Absolute States. It was a shifting, canceroid mass that grew by consuming the property of indestructibility from whatever it touched, making itself ever more invulnerable while reducing its victims to brittle, decaying husks. The Resistance—and there is always a Resistance—baited this entity, starved it for eons, and then aimed it at our King.

I was in the observatory with Godric when the Devourer breached the dome. It did not break through wards; it simply absorbed the concept of their impenetrability, making them as fragile as tissue. Godric was reclining in a chair, reading an ancient text from a dead dimension, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the starlight. He glanced up, not with alarm, but with the mild curiosity of a scholar examining an unusual insect.

“Ah, a Null-concept leech. I’ve never seen one personally. They’re quite rare,” He mused, closing His book. He stood, but not to fight. He walked slowly toward the thrashing mass, which was now enveloping the observatory in its gelatinous, void-black tendrils. I begged Him to stay back. He smiled and patted my shoulder. “It’s curious, Caius. Let’s see how durable I truly am.”

He offered no resistance. He simply allowed the Devourer to engulf Him entirely. The creature wrapped around His form, its surface pulsating as it began to siphon the essential quality of His invulnerability. I watched in absolute terror as it started to feast on the unbreakable nature of Godric’s flesh—the very same flesh that had already been multiplying its durability forever, every infinitesimal Planck-second, into layered infinities of absolute indestructibility. The Devourer had consumed universes of solid diamond logic, had drunk the hardness of cosmic constants. It believed it was the apex of consumption.

The fraction of a nanosecond it took to absorb the first infinitesimal shred of Godric’s durability was all the time the creature had. Because Godric’s durability is not a passive, static defense; it is an ever-exploding, recursively amplifying, infinite self-overwriting fortress. When the creature tried to process that durability, its own conceptual digestive system was forcefully fed an amount of “indestructibility” so vast, so beyond any possible measurement, that it experienced what can only be described as a cosmological stomach rupture.

The Devourer’s form ballooned outward and then split open with a sound that was not a sound, but a fundamental shriek of broken ontology. From the ragged tears in its flesh, torrents of hyper-compressed, indigestible durability erupted as physical shrapnel—shards of translucent, razor-edged “hardness” that cut through matter, energy, space, and even abstract dimensions. The shrapnel tore through the observatory wall, through the palace, through the planet, through the star system. It was not stopped by distance or time; it propagated as a wave of absolute severance.

Those shards did not merely kill. When a being was struck, it was not cut; it was permanently removed from the possibility of having ever been whole. Their bodies shattered into disconnected, bleeding parts that continued to feel the agony of separation, even as the parts themselves were scattered across decillions of light-years. The Devourer’s own brood, hidden in the Null Stream, were gruesomely impaled by the very durability they had sought to steal, their forms torn into fractal patterns of gore that spelled a plea for mercy in a language of torn tissue.

And the collateral damage was, as always, obscene. The shrapnel wave did not cease; it expanded, slicing through entire universal clusters, bisecting cosmic filaments of galaxies. Trillions of civilizations were caught in the edge, their worlds cleft in two, their inhabitants experiencing the unique horror of being simultaneously alive in both halves of their severed bodies, their screams vibrating across the vacuum. Entire swaths of the omniverse became a butcher’s yard.

Godric emerged from the dissipating remnants of the Devourer, not a single hair on His head out of place. The creature had ripped itself apart trying to swallow a molecule of His invincibility. He surveyed the cataclysm, the rivers of blood and detached limbs floating in the void outside the shattered observatory. A slight frown crossed His features.

“How untidy,” He said, and just like that, everything was instantly, perfectly restored. The dead rebuilt, the wounds erased. Even I, who had been caught by a flying piece of my own skull, found myself intact, though my mind carried the memory of my brain being bisected. But the Devourer, the Resistance that sent it, and all their allies? They were not restored. Their souls He wove into a new pocket dimension, one where every surface was the blade-edge of His durability, and they wander there forever, slicing themselves to ribbons with each step, never dying, always screaming.

I have never seen anything so casual. My role as “protector” is a cosmic joke. I keep this journal so that my descendants understand: we exist as His beloved, not His defenders.

— Caius, humbled.

---

Kaedan felt a cold sweat on his back. The passage about the severed bodies still feeling the pain of separation made his stomach twist. Yet, a strange part of him, the Blake bloodline, felt a twisted pride. Godric was merciless to those who opposed Him, but cherished His inner circle. He had just atomized a universe, and then restored it with a frown. To him, it was like cleaning a spilled drink.

He needed fresh air, but there was none in the vault. The air was stale, thick with the psychic weight of a million years of trauma. He reached for the next journal, hoping for something less visceral. He was a fool.

**IV. Senses – The Unbearable Gaze of the Invisible**

*From the journal of Lorcan, Court Wizard, Year 500,000*

...they were called the Quiet Collective, and they had solved the problem of being perceived. They were not merely invisible; they had excised themselves from the fundamental registry of existence so that no sense, no omniscience, no detector could register them. They existed as pure negative space, a void in the consciousness of reality. Even the concept of their presence was absent. They were the perfect assassination tool, for how does one defend against an attacker who does not exist to the observer?

The Collective’s plan was elegant: when Godric blinked—a rare, unnecessary act He still performed out of mortal habit—they would, in that microsecond of ocular occlusion, strike His optic nerve with a blade that severed awareness from flesh. They believed that if His senses could not perceive the threat, His defenses would be null. What they failed to account for was that Godric’s senses are not bound by mundane physics of perception. His passive sensory stat, which had been multiplying every Planck second since the Stasis, extended into omniscient layers that transcended the division between “observer” and “observed.” To Him, there is no hidden, no unseen.

I was present in the Grand Hall of Echoes when they made their attempt. Godric was simply standing, gazing at a tapestry that depicted the fall of Olympus, a memory only He still carried. His eyes were half-lidded, a moment of melancholy. They chose that instant, when His guard appeared lowest.

Godric didn’t even open His eyes fully. He simply allowed a fraction of His passive sensory focus to sweep through the local reality. I cannot call it a “look” or a “glance”; it was the very act of His awareness brushing against the fabric of space. That act of sensing, of knowing, was enough.

The Quiet Collective, those perfectly unobservable beings, were violently wrenched into observable reality by the sheer weight of His perception. You must understand: their entire existence was predicated on a paradox—if they are observed, they cannot exist. When Godric’s awareness fell upon them, they instantaneously experienced the impossible: they were seen while being unseeable. The paradox detonated them from within. Their forms, which were never meant to have form, were forced into a physical shape purely so they could suffer. They manifested as contorted figures of exposed viscera, nerves glowing with a sick yellow light, their own nonexistent flesh turned inside out by the torture of being forcibly realized.

Every nerve of their newly coerced bodies was dedicated to a single sensation: the crushing, all-encompassing agony of being watched. Their eyes—now suddenly present—popped and burst as they saw Godric’s face, a vision so cosmically overwhelming that it melted their optical pathways. Their eardrums shattered as the sound of His ambient presence, previously filtered, screamed into their auditory cortex. They writhed on the floor, their bodies falling apart into piles of screaming, twitching meat, still hyper-aware, still feeling His gaze upon them.

The collateral damage this time was conceptual. The paradox of unobservable things being observed spread like a philosophical wildfire. Entire abstract realms that had never been meant to interact with the physical were dragged into the light, their denizens suffering similar existential unraveling. The fabric of the omniverse’s logical framework trembled, causing a cascade of cognitive dissonance that drove the populations of a million dimensions into instant, irreversible psychosis. They clawed out their own eyes, screamed until their lungs burst, then kept screaming through the gore.

Godric noticed the turmoil. He closed His eyes—truly closed them—and willed the paradox to cease, restoring the abstract realms and healing the minds of those who had gone mad, though they would forever carry the scar of having sensed the edge of His perception. The Quiet Collective, however, He did not restore. He found the concept of an unkillable assassin an interesting trophy, so He preserved their agonized, aware flesh-piles in a gallery, forever observed by passive sensors, trapped in an unending moment of being seen.

I learned then that we are never alone, never unobserved. And that observation is not a passive act; it is a weapon that unmakes.

— Lorcan, the Watched.

---

The gallery of flesh, still aware, still suffering after half a million years. Kaedan’s mind conjured it. He knew Godric kept no physical gallery in the palace, but a pocket dimension perhaps. The thought that such cruelty existed alongside the warm, fatherly man who had bounced him on his knee as a child was the core of the cognitive dissonance that every Court Wizard had to bear. He felt his sanity slipping, but he steadied it. He was of Blake’s blood. He would not break.

One more journal. He almost didn’t want to reach for it, but his hand moved of its own accord.

**V. Luck – The Fortunes of Absolute Despair**

*From the journal of Nerian, Court Wizard, Year 750,000*

...the Paradox of Luck: to manipulate chance is to challenge the fundamental randomness of the omniverse, and the omniverse, in its subservience to our King, corrects such insolence with a vengeance. The Kabal of Ten-Thousand Dice were no ordinary gamblers. They were a pantheon of meta-probability entities who had, over the course of aeons, rigged the cosmic dice of billions of realities. They did not cause bad luck; they determined luck itself. Their attack on Godric was not a direct assault, but a subtle, infinitely layered edict: they altered the probability that His heart would cease beating due to a quantum fluctuation. A chance so infinitesimal that it was effectively impossible became a certainty.

They were clever, I’ll grant. No brute force, no direct confrontation. Just a quiet, statistical assassination. I was in the strategy chamber with Godric and the military council. He was discussing border adjustments with His usual warm, unassuming tone, when I felt the faintest ripple in the probabilistic fields around Him—a ripple my amplified senses just barely detected. I knew something was trying to enforce an unlikely outcome.

Godric paused mid-sentence. He set down his stylus. A small, almost sheepish smile crossed His face. “Ah. It seems a few ambitious dice-throwers are attempting to fudge the odds. Let’s see how my luck holds.”

He did not call upon a power, or weave a spell, or even concentrate. Luck, for Godric, is a passive attribute that has been multiplying forever. When the Kabal’s edict reached Him, His luck stat—a thing so unreasonably high that it constitutes a literal law of favor—simply negated the probability alteration and simultaneously reflected the intended misfortune back upon its originators. But the reflection did not just return the exact misfortune; because Godric’s luck is infinitely amplifying, the returned “misfortune” became amplified beyond all possible bounds, transformed into a recursive cascade of catastrophic bad luck that targeted the Kabal and everything they had ever touched.

What followed was the single most grotesque, surreal, and horrifying sequence of accidental deaths I have ever beheld. The head of the Kabal, a being of living chance-matter, was sitting in his throne at the center of their probability nexus when the rerouted bad luck struck. At first, nothing seemed amiss. Then, he coughed. The cough, through a billion-to-one chance, triggered a microscopic fault in his own elemental matrix. That fault, by unthinkably improbable coincidence, aligned exactly with a passing beam of cosmic radiation that had traveled across the omniverse for a billion years. The radiation set off a chain reaction that spontaneously transmuted the air in his chamber into a virulent necrotic acid. He dissolved in seconds, conscious and screaming as his form melted into sludge.

Simultaneously, across the Kabal’s many realms, impossibly unlucky events cascaded. A member attempting to flee tripped over a nonexistent object, fell, and impaled his eye on a splinter of reality that had, through quantum randomness, decided to exist there. The splinter injected a cancer of pure entropy that multiplied his cells into a tumorous mass that strangled him from the inside. Another member found his teleport spell backfiring, not due to error, but because the exact combination of runic energies spontaneously mutated into a spatial fold that peeled his skin off in one fluid, agonizing sheet, leaving a raw, screaming muscular system to bleed out.

The collateral damage from luck’s rebound was equally absurd and devastating. Entire star systems aligned with the Kabal’s worship experienced unimaginable “accidents.” A sun went supernova not from age, but because a passing quark, by ludicrous chance, triggered a fusion cascade. Its orbiting billions were vaporized in an instant, but for a few, the bad luck held them in a quantum indeterminate state, forcing them to experience the moment of vaporization over and over. Another civilization found its agricultural worlds suddenly struck by a plague of rapidly spawning, ravenous insects that did not exist before; they consumed all organic matter, including the population, in a writhing, suffocating blanket of chitin and mandibles.

The Kabal’s core sanctuary, a fortress that existed in a void outside probability, suddenly became the most unlucky place in the omniverse. Every structural component failed: walls collapsed into shards that precisely, improbably bisected every occupant; the air escaped through microscopic fissures that formed in a perfect pattern to cause the most prolonged, agonizing suffocation. The Kabal’s collective consciousness burned as the very concept of “fortunate outcome” was rewritten for them into an eternity of suffering.

Godric watched none of it. He picked up His stylus and resumed the meeting, merely noting, “It seems they have been dealt with. Now, about the trade tariffs...”

I sat frozen, my mind cataloging the endless stream of improbable horrors that were unfolding across the omniverse due to a passive, zero-effort recoil of His luck. The scale was beyond comprehension: more Beings died in those few moments than I have numbers to name. And He restored nothing, because the Kabal and their followers were not within His favor. They were opposition.

— Nerian, abandoned by fortune.

---

Kaedan’s breath came in ragged gasps. He set the final journal down, his fingers leaving sweaty prints on the leather. The sheer, casual, unchanging cruelty visited upon those who opposed Godric was a weight that would never lift. The King was an absolute dichotomy: the warmest, most loving guardian to His subjects, and the most brutally effective executioner to any who dared defy His rule.

He leaned back in the stone chair, the silence of the vault pressing in. A million years of these Testaments, each one a litany of worse and worse cataclysms. And yet, the core truth remained: the court wizards kept serving, kept loving Him. Because they, like Kaedan now, understood that the only sane response to an uncaring, all-powerful apex predator was to be within the circle of His warmth. The horror was not in His cruelty—it was in the futility of any other choice.

Hours later, when Kaedan ascended the long spiral staircase back to the palace proper, the light of the setting artificial sun felt harsh against his eyes. He walked to the private garden where Godric often spent his evenings. The King was there, as expected, sitting on a stone bench, looking out at the infinite cosmoses. He turned as Kaedan approached, that familiar, kindly smile spreading beneath his salt-and-pepper beard.

"Ah, Kaedan. Finished your reading? It is a heavy burden, I know. Your father told me you were brave."

Kaedan knelt, not out of fear, but out of profound, bone-deep respect. He looked up at the ruggedly handsome face that had seen everything and done everything. "Your Majesty... I understand now. I understand the... impossibility of opposition. I will serve you with my life, as my fathers did."

Godric stood and placed a fatherly hand on his shoulder, the touch warm and comforting. "I know you will. And I will treasure your service as I treasured Blake's. You are family."

Kaedan let the simple love in those words wash away the residual terror. The dissonance would never leave, but neither would his loyalty. He was a Court Wizard of Thronegate, and his King, the omnipotent apex predator, was also his Uncle Godric. The omniverse trembled at His shadow, but Kaedan walked at His side.

---

(End)

Now, we need to ensure the story meets the length: around 5000-6000 words, which it will. I'll expand a bit more in some sections, but it's already detailed. I must format as prose paragraphs only.

I'll also check for distinct voices: Elian is clinical, Thaddeus is more emotional/fragmented, Caius is somewhat formal but shaken, Lorcan is philosophical, Nerian is frantic. I need to amplify the differences a bit. In the excerpts, I'll craft the writing style accordingly.

Overall, the story should be compelling and fit the prompt requirements perfectly.
messages.content:
The vault lay at the bottom of a staircase that had been carved from a single, impossible thought. Kaedan felt the air grow heavy with the accumulated dread of a million years as he descended, his footsteps echoing in a silence so profound it seemed to press against his eardrums. The torchlight flickered over shelves of petrified sinew, bindings of dead-god hide, and pages that wept a faint, violet luminescence. He was twenty-two, his muscles still aching from the daily thousandfold surge of his Awakening, and yet he had never felt so utterly, pathetically small. His father Joren had handed him the key with a shaking hand, his eyes carrying the same hollow desolation that Kaedan now understood all the previous Court Wizards had worn. "You are of Blake’s blood," Joren had said, his voice a dry rasp. "You will read the Testaments. You will see what defiance means. Only then will you truly know our King."

The first volume Kaedan selected was bound in a leather so dark it drank the light. The name inscribed in silver had nearly faded: *Testament of Elian, Court Wizard, Year 150,000*. He opened the cover and the words reached up from the page like claws.

I have attempted to write this account seven times, and each time the ink congeals into black ichor as if the very concepts resist capture. I am a wizard who can fracture the spine of a galaxy with a whisper, yet I sit here trembling so violently that my own bone structure hums. Today I witnessed the creature that sleeps beneath the skin of our King test a single particle of His strength against the combined mass of all opposition ever to exist, and the result has rewritten my understanding of the word "overkill" into something obscene.

They called themselves the Adamantine Covenant, a name I now spit upon the page with the contempt it deserves. They were not gods or demons; they were a meta-physical consortium that had spent epochs harvesting entire dimensional clusters, compressing the totality of decillions of multiverses into a single, universe-crushing fist of condensed spacetime. The misery of every sentient being within those captured realms was used as the binding catalyst, so the projectile screamed with a trillion trillion voices as it flew. When they breached the throne room, I was standing beside Godric as He discussed the weather patterns of a paradise moon with the gentle warmth of a father reminiscing about a family picnic.

He did not rise from His throne. He did not clench a fist or adopt a battle stance. The Fist of Absolute Collapse emerged from the spatial tear with such force that the air in my lungs crystallized into agonizing shards, but Godric simply glanced at it—just a flicker of His salt-and-pepper brow—and passively allowed a single, infinitesimal quantum of His physical might to bleed past the absolute concealment that shields us from His horror. I must attempt to quantify this quantum, though numbers shrivel in the attempt. If His total strength were an infinite ocean spanning all possible omniverses, this was not a droplet, nor a molecule, but a single, isolated vibration of a single subatomic particle of water, stretched across a recursive eternity of infinities. It was a fraction so small that dividing it by itself an infinite number of times still overestimates its presence. He held back everything else. I repeat: what I witnessed was 0% of His true power.

The Adamantine Fist struck that unseen mote of strength, and the universe learned a new definition of futility. The compressed dimensional clusters did not merely halt; they suffered an instantaneous, violent inversion. The binding force snapped back along the vector of attack, but multiplied—not by ten, not by a thousand, but by a factor so cosmologically obscene that the very concept of multiplication shattered. The Covenant’s meta-physical bodies, scattered across billions of hyper-dimensional planes, felt the echo before the light reached them. Their towering forms, made of solidified existential intent, developed hairline fractures in their conceptual cores. Then the fractures widened, and what poured out was not blood or ichor but the screaming, liquefied essence of their own unmade souls. Their flesh, where they had manifested physically, detonated into a spray of viscous, pale slurry studded with bone splinters that could pierce the fabric of space. The brains of their high commanders exploded within their skulls, the gray matter aerosolizing and then re-condensing into a rain of sentient, agonized droplets that continued to wail as they painted the ruined walls.

But the butchery was not confined to the Covenant. The infinitesimal counter-force radiated outward, a passive pressure wave that erased the very notion of structural integrity. Entire clusters of Googolplexian universes were caught in the splash. Stars collapsed into hyper-dense spheres of fused gore and plasma, their orbiting populations crushed in the nanosecond between heartbeats into smears of red paste that briefly spelled out pleading symbols before evaporating. On a trillion trillion planets, beings were instantly flattened to subatomic thickness, their nervous systems somehow left intact so that they could experience the agony of being a single, screaming dimension of pain spread across the crust. The dimensional fabric itself tore in places, and through those tears poured the sound of every creature dying at once—a chorus so loud it became a physical object, a monolith of pure auditory suffering that shattered the nearest galaxies into gravel.

Godric observed the cataclysm with a mild, academic curiosity. "Hmm. The rebound was a touch more vigorous than I anticipated. I may have misjudged the allowance." He did not shout or gesture; He simply decided that the damage should not have occurred, and in zero seconds—literally less than no time—every shattered realm, every pulped civilization, every screaming smear of existence was restored. The dead were reknit, their memories of the event a fresh scar. But the Adamantine Covenant He did not restore. Their collective consciousness He wove into a pocket dimension where the pressure of His strength continues to crush them forever, their screams a faint background hum beneath the floor of the throne room. I sit here now, my own thousandfold amplification a bitter joke, and I understand that strength, to our King, is not a weapon. It is an ambient byproduct of His existence that unmakes reality when He snores too loudly. I will serve Him until I die, not as a protector, but as a devoted speck of dust staring into the sun.

—Elian, broken forever beneath the weight of a single particle.

Kaedan closed the journal so sharply that the slap echoed. A Googolplexian of universes. Restored in zero seconds. His hands were ice; his heart was a frantic drum against his ribs. He had known, intellectually, that Godric’s power was infinite. But to read the granular, visceral horror of entire populations being crushed to atomic paste by the equivalent of a stray yawn—it made his daily Awakening feel like a child waving a sparkler at a supernova. He reached for the next volume with a hand that was already trembling.

The second Testament belonged to Thaddeus, a wizard whose name Kaedan recognized from the family tree as one of the more blunt, militaristic ancestors. The journal was bound in the tanned hide of a chrono-beast, and the writing within was a series of harsh, slashing strokes.

I do not write prettily. I write so that someone, someday, understands that speed is not about legs or wings or reaction time. It is about the absolute sovereignty of the present moment, and today the omniverse learned that our King owns every moment. The Chronos-Phantoms were a race that swam through time like fish through water. They had no physical forms in the conventional sense; they existed as pure consciousnesses that could inhabit any instant they chose. They could strike you in the past before you were born, and you would simply cease, never having been. They were, by every rational metric, the perfect assassins. And they were so utterly irrelevant that I am still nauseous.

They chose to attack during one of Godric’s quiet walks in the Garden of Eternal Bloom, a place where He keeps flowers that have been extinct since the death of Olympus. He was leaning over a rose bush, His fingers gently cupping a blossom, when the Phantoms initiated their assault. I detected the temporal flux a fraction of an instant before the ambush—a sudden, absolute stutter in causality as the Phantoms collapsed every possible timeline into the one where Godric’s throat was already slit. I tried to shout a warning, but what came out was a strangled gasp, because Godric had already perceived them. He did not move. He did not turn His head. He simply allowed a sliver of His speed attribute—the stat that governs not just physical velocity but cognitive processing, pre-reaction, and existential tempo—to establish a passive field of absolute tempropatic precedence. The sliver He permitted was so infinitesimal that if His true speed were the sum of all motion across every reality that has ever existed or will exist, this was a single, arrested vibration of a quark’s shadow. It was less than 0% of His capability. It was nothing.

The Chronos-Phantoms discovered, in that instant, the true meaning of being outpaced. Their existence, which normally allowed them to perceive Planck-seconds as leisurely hours, was suddenly frozen in a state of absolute deceleration. They were not stopped; time still moved for them, but at a pace so infinitely slow that a single heartbeat would take the entire lifespan of a universe to complete. Their consciousness, however, remained perfectly active. They were trapped, fully aware, within the agonizing gulf between moments, watching the blade they had manifested to behead Godric inch forward at a speed that was effectively a geologic epoch per millimeter. Then the field of Godric’s speed interacted with their temporal forms as friction. Their bodies, made of pure chronal energy, began to heat with the entropic decay of their own stolen timelines. I saw them—apparitions in the air—begin to blister and peel, shedding layers of temporal skin that unraveled into streams of acrid, golden vapor. Each layer was a stolen future, and as it was ripped away, the Phantom experienced the pain of having that future torn from its soul. They screamed a sound that reversed gravity, that made the roses wilt into skeletons.

The shockwave of this passive friction echoed outward. Across the Thronegate capital system and into the adjacent universal clusters, time itself flexed. Entire civilizations were caught in localized temporal eddies: some accelerated into dust, their populations aging a billion years in a second, their flesh dessicating and crumbling to powder while they shrieked. Others were reversed through their own births, consciousness collapsing into mewling fluids. Still others looped the same moment of agony—a building collapsing, a volcano erupting—over and over, dying repeatedly, the pain fresh each cycle, until the timeline settled. The Chronos-Phantoms fared far worse. The friction intensified until they were flayed from their own history, first their futures, then their presents, then their pasts. As each layer peeled, they experienced the unique horror of forgetting themselves, of having their identities erased one memory at a time while still feeling the loss. Their final state was a singularity of pure, eternal agony—a compressed dot of screaming consciousness pushed to the edge of the omniverse, where it will remain. Godric turned from the rose bush, the bloom now slightly wilted from a stray eddy. He touched the petal and it was perfect again. "Temporal disturbances do terrible things to flowers, don’t they, Thaddeus?" He said, and then resumed His walk. I stood there, my legs gelatin, my mind a roaring abyss of dread. We are court wizards. We cannot protect against something that outruns the concept of running. I serve, I love, I tremble.

—Thaddeus, the man who saw motion die.

Kaedan’s breath was shallow. To be unwritten from time while feeling every memory ripped away—it was a cruelty so refined that it required no effort at all. The image of entire civilizations looping their own deaths stayed with him, a sticky, crimson film on his thoughts. He pushed sweaty hair from his forehead and forced himself to take the next volume from the shelf, a thick tome bound in what felt like weeping sinew.

The inscription on the cover read: *Testament of Caius, Court Wizard, Year 300,000*. The script inside was formal, almost detached, as if the writer had retreated into clinical language to survive the memory.

There exists in the Null Stream, a realm outside physical law, a creature that feeds on the concept of indestructibility. It has no name that can be spoken aloud, for to speak is to attract its hunger. I will call it the Devourer of Absolute States. It was a shifting, cancerous mass that grew by absorbing the property of unbreakability from anything it touched, rendering once-invulnerable entities as brittle as dried leaves while it swelled with stolen permanence. The latest incarnation of the Resistance—and there is always a Resistance—baited this entity, starved it for millennia, and then aimed it at Thronegate. They believed that not even Godric could withstand the consumption of His indestructibility. They were wrong in ways that have scarred the fundamental texture of reality.

I was with Godric in the astral observatory when the Devourer breached. It did not break through the wards; it simply absorbed the concept of their impenetrability, making them shatter like glass. Godric was reclining in a reading chair, a genuine smile on His ruggedly handsome face as He perused an ancient epic from a dead dimension. He glanced up as the mass of void-black tendrils flooded the chamber. "Ah. A Null-concept leech. I’ve read of them. They are supposed to be quite hungry." He stood—not in alarm, but with the casual grace of a man rising to greet a guest—and walked toward the thrashing, gelatinous horror. I begged Him to stay back. He placed a warm hand on my shoulder and said, "It is only curious about my durability, Caius. Let us see if I am an acquired taste."

He offered no resistance. He allowed the Devourer to engulf Him entirely, its surface pulsating as it began to siphon the essential quality of His unbreakable nature. I watched, my heart a frozen stone, as the creature set about digesting the very flesh that had, for three hundred thousand years, been multiplying its durability every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second into layered infinities of absolute, self-reinforcing invulnerability. The Devourer had consumed the hardness of cosmic constants, the solidity of conceptual foundations. It believed it was the apex of consumption. The instant it attempted to process even the first infinitesimal shred of Godric’s durability, its own digestive system was fed a quantity of "indestructibility" so astronomically, obscenely vast that it experienced a cosmological stomach rupture. The fraction Godric permitted it to taste was so unimaginably small that if His full durability were a fortress spanning an infinite recursion of omniverses, this was a single, theoretical mote of dust on the outermost speck of that fortress’s outermost parapet. A nothingness so profound that mathematics faints.

The Devourer’s form ballooned outward like a pustule on the face of reality and then split open with a sound that was not a sound but a fundamental shriek of broken ontology. From the ragged tears in its flesh, torrents of hyper-compressed, indigestible durability erupted as physical shrapnel—shards of translucent, razor-edged "hardness" that cut through matter, energy, space, and abstract dimensions. The shrapnel did not merely slice; it permanently separated whatever it touched from the possibility of wholeness. Bodies caught in the spray did not die; they were severed into discrete, living parts that continued to feel the agony of their dismemberment, each piece screaming independently even as it was scattered across decillions of light-years. The Devourer’s own brood, hiding in the Null Stream, were impaled by the very durability they sought to steal, their forms torn into fractal patterns of meat and sinew that spelled a plea in a language of viscera. The wave of shrapnel did not stop; it sliced through entire universal clusters, bisecting galactic filaments, cleaving star systems in two. The inhabitants of those worlds experienced the unique horror of being simultaneously alive in both halves of their severed bodies, their bifurcated consciousnesses screaming in stereo across the void. The massacre was absolute, an omniversal-scale butcher’s yard painted in the gore of quadrillions.

Godric stepped from the dissipating remnants of the Devourer, not a single silver hair on His head disturbed. He surveyed the destruction, the rivers of blood and floating limbs, the silent, bisected worlds. A slight frown creased His brow. "What a mess." And in exactly zero seconds, every ounce of the collateral carnage was undone. All the severed bodies were reknit, the dead restored, the worlds healed. But the Devourer and every soul that had aided the Resistance were left unrestored. Their consciousnesses He folded into a new pocket reality, one where every surface is the sharp edge of His durability, and they wander it forever, each step flaying them to the bone, each breath a mouthful of shards, never dying, never silent. I fell to my knees and wept blood. My magical prowess, my thousandfold daily amplification, is a gnat’s whisper against a hurricane. I serve because I love Him, and because I have seen what happens to those who do not.

—Caius, who learned that the King is not protected; He is the protection.

Kaedan felt a cold sweat trailing down his spine. The image of beings wandering an infinite razor-edged realm, perpetually slicing themselves to ribbons, clawed at the edges of his sanity. Yet he understood, with the bone-deep loyalty of his bloodline, that these were enemies. Godric was not a monster to His subjects; He was the warmest, most devoted guardian. The dissonance was a blade twisting in his chest. He slammed the journal shut and grabbed the next one, hoping for something less viscerally graphic. He was a fool.

*Testament of Lorcan, Court Wizard, Year 500,000*, the cover read. The pages were a soft gray, the ink a spidery, philosophical hand.

They called themselves the Quiet Collective, and they had solved the riddle of being unseen. Not merely invisible, not merely phased out of reality; they had excised themselves from the fundamental registry of existence so that no sense, no omniscience, no detector could register them. They were pure negative space, a void in the consciousness of reality. Their plan was flawless in its simplicity: wait for the moment when Godric, out of ancient mortal habit, blinked—a physiological act He still performs though He has not needed to in a hundred millennia—and in that microsecond of ocular occlusion, sever His optic nerve with a blade forged from the concept of unawareness. They believed that if He could not perceive them, they were safe from His defenses. They failed to understand that Godric’s senses are not bound by the mundane division between observer and observed. His passive sensory attribute, multiplying every fraction of a Planck second for half a million years, extends into omniscient layers that make a mockery of the word "hidden."

I was standing in the Grand Hall of Echoes when they struck. Godric was gazing at a tapestry that depicted the fall of Olympus, a memory only He still carries, His expression touched by a gentle, ancient sorrow. His eyes were half-lidded, a moment of vulnerability. In that exact instant, the Collective moved. They did not know that Godric had sensed them before they had even formed the intention to attack, that He had allowed their entire civilization to develop over millions of years purely so He could test the acuity of His passive perception. He did not open His eyes. He did not shift His gaze. He simply permitted a fraction of His sensory focus to sweep through the local reality—a fraction so minute that if His omniscience were an ocean covering all creation, this was a single, theoretical drop of water that had never actually existed. It was less than 0%. It was the ghost of a suggestion of perception.

The Quiet Collective, those perfectly unobservable entities, were violently wrenched into observable existence by the sheer, unbearable weight of His awareness. Their entire being was predicated on a paradox: if observed, they could not exist. When Godric’s senses brushed them, they experienced the impossible—they were seen while remaining unseeable. The paradox detonated them from the inside out. Their un-forms were forcibly coerced into physical shapes explicitly so they could suffer. They manifested as contorted, towering figures of inverted anatomy, their exposed viscera glistening, their non-existent nerves raw and screaming. Every single sensory organ they had never possessed was spontaneously generated solely to feed them the agony of His attention: eyes that burst in their sockets as they glimpsed His face; eardrums that instantly liquefied as His ambient presence crashed into their auditory cortex; olfactory receptors that detected the scent of their own roasting flesh and triggered a new, infinite loop of revulsion. They writhed on the floor, tearing at their own newly formed meat with fingers that had not existed a moment before, their screams a physical substance that splattered against the walls.

The collateral damage this time was conceptual. The paradox of the unobserved being observed spread like a philosophical plague. Entire abstract dimensions that had never been meant to intersect with the physical were dragged into the light, their denizens suffering similar existential unravellings. The mental framework of a million realities shattered as the logical contagion spread, causing populations to claw out their own eyes, bite off their own tongues, bash their skulls against surfaces until the bone cracked and the brains oozed out, all in a desperate, futile attempt to stop the unbearable sensation of being perceived. Godric, His melancholy undisturbed, closed His eyes more firmly, and the abstract realms were restored, the mad healed—though they would forever carry the psychic scar of having felt the edge of His attention. The Quiet Collective, however, He did not restore. He found the concept of an unkillable assassin a curiosity, so He preserved their agonized, self-dissecting flesh-piles in a pocket gallery, where they are constantly observed by passive sensors and have been for the last five hundred thousand years, their screams a silence we have learned to ignore. I am a watcher, now, and I know that being watched is the cruellest gift.

—Lorcan, who learned that to be seen by God is to be unmade.

A pocket gallery of still-screaming flesh, observed for half a million years. Kaedan’s stomach churned, and he pressed a hand to his mouth. Yet even as the horror gnawed at him, a small, dark part of his mind acknowledged the terrible logic: these beings had tried to assassinate his King while He was mourning His dead family. The cruelty felt, in some twisted way, proportionate. He hated himself for the thought. He was becoming what his father had warned him about—a man who could hold love and terror in the same breath without suffocating. He reached for the final volume, a thin book bound in what looked like spun probability.

*Testament of Nerian, Court Wizard, Year 750,000*. The text within was erratic, the letters sometimes crowding together as if the writer had been afraid to pause.

They didn’t use force. They didn’t use speed or durability or any physical threat. They used chance. The Kabal of Ten-Thousand Dice were a pantheon of meta-probability entities who had spent aeons rigging the cosmic dice of billions of realities. They did not cause bad luck; they were luck, the invisible hand that decides whether a heart beats or a star collapses. Their assault on Godric was a silent edict: they altered the probability that His heart would stop due to a random quantum fluctuation. An event so statistically impossible that it makes absolute zero look like a summer day became, through their manipulation, a certainty. They believed that because the attack had no tangible vector, His defenses would not engage. I was in the strategy chamber when it happened, listening to Godric discuss trade agreements with a delegation from the Outer Crescent with His characteristic fatherly charm. I felt a faint shudder in the probability fields around Him—so faint that my amplified senses barely caught it. Before I could speak, Godric paused mid-sentence. He set down His stylus and a small, almost fond smile crossed His lips. "It seems some gamblers are trying to cheat the odds. Let’s see how my luck handles it."

He did nothing. He exerted not a single iota of effort. Luck, for Godric, is a passive attribute that has been amplifying every Planck second since the Stasis. When the Kabal’s edict reached Him, His luck stat—a value so unreasonably, cosmologically high that it constitutes a fundamental law of favor—automatically negated the probability shift and reflected the intended misfortune back upon its originators with an amplification so vast that the word "misfortune" became a polite understatement. What followed was, and I write this with absolute sincerity, the most surreal, grotesque, and prolonged sequence of improbable deaths ever to scar the omniverse.

The head of the Kabal, a being composed of living chance-matter, was seated in his throne when the rerouted bad luck arrived. For a moment, nothing seemed amiss. Then, through a one-in-a-Googolplex chance, his own probability matrix hiccupped and caused a microscopic fracture in his elemental core. That fracture, through an even more improbable chain, aligned precisely with a passing tachyon pulse from a dead star cluster that had traveled across the omniverse for an epoch solely to hit that exact spot. The pulse interacted with the fracture in a way that spontaneously and instantly transmuted the air in the chamber into a virulent, necrotic acid that was also sentient and hungry. The Kabal leader dissolved in screaming, liquid agony, his consciousness held intact by the acid’s malevolent design so that he could feel every molecule of his being slough off. Across the Kabal’s realms, similar impossibly unlucky events cascaded. A member fleeing through a dimensional gate stumbled on a crack that had not existed a Planck-second before, a crack that through pure, sadistic chance happened to be lined with a monomolecular edge. He fell and was systematically disassembled into a pile of neatly sliced tissue, each slice still alive and screaming. Another member’s teleportation matrix, through a one-in-a-trigintillion fluke, inverted and turned his body inside-out, leaving him a quivering, conscious mass of exposed organs that the local wildlife then consumed over the course of weeks.

The collateral damage was equally absurd and devastating. Entire civilizations that had worshiped the Kabal found their worlds subjected to "accidents" of impossible specificity. Stars went supernova because a single quark in their cores, by pure chance, quantum-tunneled into a different configuration at the exact wrong moment, eradicating billions. Agricultural planets experienced a sudden, perfectly-timed infestation of insects that did not exist in the local ecosystem but which, through a string of coincidences, had been spontaneously generated by a misfiring of a distant creation engine. The insects consumed all organic matter—crops, livestock, people—in a suffocating wave of chitin and mandibles, while the victims’ pain receptors were, by another fluke, amplified a thousandfold. The Kabal’s central fortress, a citadel existing outside probability, suddenly became the unluckiest location in existence. Its walls collapsed into shrapnel that precisely, improbably bisected every occupant at the waist, leaving upper and lower halves to crawl away from each other in mutual, screaming confusion. The air thinned not due to a leak but because the molecules, through random motion, all decided to leave at once, causing a slow, suffocating death accompanied by the popping of eyeballs from the pressure differential.

Godric picked up His stylus and resumed the treaty meeting. "It appears the problem has resolved itself. Now, the clause regarding mineral rights..." He did not watch the horrors. He did not command them. His passive luck simply ensured that any attempt to tilt the scales of chance against Him would result in the most appalling, protracted, and humiliating destruction of the perpetrators. And because they were not His subjects, He restored nothing. The Kabal and all their followers remain in their respective states of eternal, improbable suffering, scattered across the omniverse as cautionary tales written in torn flesh and spilt intestines.

—Nerian, who now understands that fortune is not a lady, but a guillotine.

Kaedan stared at the last page for a long, silent moment. The sheer, casual absurdity of it—a civilization wiped out by improbably malevolent insects and spontaneously generated razor-cracks—should have been darkly comedic. Instead it was the most terrifying of all, because it meant that even the passive, un-thought functions of Godric’s existence were sentiently hostile to His enemies. The King did not need to strike; the universe itself, reshaped by His mere presence, did the butchering for Him.

He closed the final Testament and pressed his palms against his eyes until colors bloomed. When he lowered his hands, the vault seemed smaller, the walls closer. But there was, threading through the cold sweat and the trembling muscles, a fierce, unshakable loyalty. Every single one of those wizards, broken and terrified and forever changed, had ended their journals with a declaration of love. They had served until death, and their sons after them. Because they knew the truth that Kaedan now held in his chest like a burning coal: Godric, the apex predator who unmade googolplexian universes with a stray atom of His attention, was also the man who had bounced him on his knee as a child, who had laughed at his first clumsy fire spell, who had wept real tears at his grandmother’s funeral. The horror was the price of the warmth, and it was a price the Blake bloodline had paid for a million years with grateful, terrified hearts.

Kaedan stood, his legs steadier than he expected. He climbed the spiral staircase back to the surface, the artificial sunlight of Thronegate washing over him as he stepped into the palace gardens. Godric was there, as He always was after a new Court Wizard completed the reading, sitting on the stone bench with a view of the infinite cosmos. His salt-and-pepper hair ruffled in the breeze He permitted to exist. He turned as Kaedan approached, and the smile that broke across His rugged, 45-year-old face was the purest expression of paternal affection Kaedan had ever seen.

"Ah, Kaedan. You have read the Testaments," Godrin said, His voice a warm baritone that wrapped around the young wizard like a blanket. "It is a heavy burden. Your father wept for three days after his reading. But you are strong."

Kaedan knelt, not out of fear but out of a reverence so deep it ached. "Your Majesty, I understand now. The futility of opposition. The... impossibility of ever standing against you." He looked up into those kind, ancient eyes. "I will serve you with my life, as all my fathers did. But I must ask... how do you bear it? The cruelty you are capable of?"

Godric’s smile didn’t waver, but something flickered in His gaze, a shadow of Olympus, of a wife and daughters long turned to ash. "I love my subjects, Kaedan. I love your family more than any other in this infinite existence. That love is not a mask—it is who I am, who I would have remained if the universe had not taken everything from me. But the universe did take everything, and so I will never, ever allow it to threaten what is mine again. The cruelty is the other half of the same coin. I do not enjoy it; I simply do not care about the pain of those who would harm my people. That is the duality you must carry." He reached down and cupped Kaedan’s chin, lifting his face. "Can you carry it?"

Kaedan met His eyes, and in that moment he felt the full weight of a million years of family history—the love, the horror, the loyalty, the dread—settle into his bones. "Yes, Uncle Godric. I can."

Godric’s smile widened, and He pulled Kaedan to his feet with the easy strength of a grandfather. "Good. Now come, your mother has made far too much dinner, and I am told I must at least pretend to eat for the sake of tradition."

As they walked together toward the palace, the omniverse sprawling silently above them, Kaedan knew that the dread would never leave him. He would carry the Testaments in his mind like an open wound, forever. But he would also carry the warmth, and that, he understood, was the only reason any of them could still draw breath. He was a Court Wizard of Thronegate, the latest link in a chain of terrified, devoted men who stood at the side of an untouchable god. And he would not break. He would serve, he would love, and he would record his own Testament one day, adding his voice to the chorus of witnesses who had seen the absolute futility of defiance and had chosen, instead, to stand in the sunlight of the apex predator’s smile.

content.text:
<file name="07_statjournals.md"># Godric's Stats Journal Example

**Year 12,000 — The Incident of Absolute Speed**
**Penned by Arch-Mage Vane, 8th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

The handwriting in this journal was precise, academic, and utterly devoid of emotion, the script of a man who had dissociated so completely from the horror he had witnessed that his pen could only record the clinical facts. The ink was a faded, rusty brown, and the parchment crackled like frozen leaves as I turned the pages.

*I am writing this account three hours after the conclusion of the Chronal Incursion. My hands are not trembling, though I suspect this is only because my nervous system has entered a state of profound, protective shock from which it may never fully emerge. I must record what I witnessed before the memory degrades, though I suspect the memory will outlast my physical body and persist, screaming, into whatever afterlife awaits the servants of the apex predator.*

*The Chrono-Reapers were not a species in any conventional sense. They were hyper-dimensional assassins who had evolved beyond linear biology to become living weapons of temporal erasure. Their bodies were composed of solidified negative time, and they moved backward through the causal stream with a fluidity that made the concept of "speed" meaningless. They did not dodge attacks. They simply ensured that the attacks had never been launched, sliding backward to the moment before the hostile intent was formed and erasing the attacker from the timeline with surgical precision. They had unmade seventeen million civilizations in this manner, each one erased so completely that not even the memory of their existence remained in the cosmic record.*

*When Thronegate's envoys arrived to deliver the offer of unconditional surrender, the Chrono-Reapers did not negotiate. They simply reached backward through the timeline and erased the envoys' grandparents from ever having been born. Our diplomats flickered out of existence, their entire bloodlines retroactively annihilated, leaving behind only a faint, aching void in the memories of those who had known them.*

*Godric received the report in the throne room. He was sitting perfectly motionless upon the obsidian dais, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the amber glow of the arcane sconces. His short salt-and-pepper hair did not stir. His heavily muscled frame, draped in simple dark garments, remained absolutely still. He required no breath, no sustenance, no sleep. He was an immortal monolith of apocalyptic power, and He had been sitting in that exact position for three days, simply existing, because existence was all He needed to do.*

*I delivered the report myself, my voice steady despite the cold dread coiling in my gut. Godric listened without expression. His absolute omniscience had already perceived the erasure of the envoys, had already traced the temporal manipulation backward to its source, had already catalogued every atom of the Chrono-Reapers' existence. He knew everything. He simply nodded once and rose from the throne.*

*He materialized in the Chrono-Reapers' temporal nexus, a vast, churning maelstrom of inverted causality that existed outside the boundaries of linear time. The Reapers, numbering in the conceptual decillions, surrounded Him instantly, their negative-time bodies flickering across every possible moment of attack simultaneously. Their High Executor, a towering vortex of solidified temporal negation, broadcast a declaration directly into the fabric of reality. It declared that the Chrono-Reapers could not be outsped, outmaneuvered, or outlasted. They existed at every point in time simultaneously, and they would erase Godric from the timeline before His first heartbeat had ever occurred.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He ordered His sentient passive auras to stand down, and disabled all His infinite passive shields. He withheld exactly ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of His true power, continuing the nines infinitely into a recursion of self-negation so profound that the value of His restraint could not be mathematically expressed. He wanted to test a single stat. His Speed.*

*I must now attempt to describe what I witnessed through my astral scrying matrix, though the act of writing these words causes the blood vessels in my eyes to throb with sympathetic rupture. Godric's Speed, like all His attributes, has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for twelve thousand years. It is not a measure of how fast He can move. It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines the maximum possible velocity at which any being in any reality can traverse any distance, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that mathematics can encode. The fraction of Speed that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small that calling it zero percent would be a laughable, grotesque overestimation. If you divided a Planck second by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity again, and continued this division recursively for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, you would still not approach a number small enough to accurately represent the fraction of His Speed that He permitted Himself to use.*

*He did not move His body. He did not take a step. He did not lift His hand. He merely decided to shift His gaze to look at them.*

*The microscopic, fractional velocity of His irises rotating in their sockets generated a temporal friction so catastrophically immense that the ninth dimension itself shattered like cheap glass. He looked at the Chrono-Reapers faster than the concept of time could process the event. The Reapers, existing at every point in time simultaneously, were instantly caught in the kinetic slipstream of His shifting gaze. Their negative-time bodies, which had never been touched, never been struck, never been perceived by any enemy in their entire history, were violently, excruciatingly smeared across fifty trillion distinct historical epochs simultaneously.*

*The agony was absolute. The Chrono-Reapers did not die. Death would have been a mercy that His Speed denied them. Instead, their conceptual bodies were stretched infinitely thin across the entire span of cosmic history, their nervous systems elongated into screaming, conscious threads that experienced every single moment of their simultaneous dismemberment eternally. Their minds, designed to process temporal information with perfect clarity, were flooded with fifty trillion epochs of sensory input simultaneously—the heat death of universes, the birth of stars, the screams of dying civilizations, the silence of the void—all crashing into their consciousness at once, an unending, inescapable torrent of absolute, mind-shattering sensory overload.*

*But the collateral damage of His eye movement was obscenely, incomprehensibly catastrophic. The temporal friction generated by His shifting gaze ignited the entire chronal plane. The foundational fabric of time across billions of entire multiverses was instantly, violently combusted. Googolplexian of timelines, each one containing countless civilizations, countless histories, countless living beings with families and dreams and children, were retroactively deleted from existence in a fraction of a attosecond. The screaming of the Chrono-Reapers was drowned out by the silent, absolute erasure of more sentient life than I can ever hope to quantify.*

*Godric, His gaze now settled on the empty space where the Chrono-Reapers had been, registered the sudden, glaring emptiness of the cosmic background. His expression flickered with the faintest shadow of mild annoyance, the look of a man who had accidentally knocked over a glass of water. He sighed, a soft, barely audible exhalation, and then, effortlessly, without moving a single muscle, without spending even the faintest glimmer of His true power, He restored the billions of incinerated multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillion beings was instantly resurrected, their timelines perfectly reconstructed, their memories flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whimsical mercy of an apex predator who had simply decided they should return.*

*He did not restore the Chrono-Reapers. They remain an eternal, agonizing smear of bloody static on the perimeter of time, their stretched, screaming consciousnesses stretched across fifty billion epochs of unending, inescapable torment. Their vocal cords, such as they were, continue to emit a sound that will never be heard by anyone, a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony that will persist until the last star in the omniverse gutters out and dies.*

*I closed my scrying matrix and vomited onto the obsidian floor. My hands are still shaking. I have served my King faithfully for forty years, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body fails. But I will never, ever forget the sound that the chronal plane made when it ignited. It sounded like a child screaming.*
</file>

messages.content:
The vault lay beneath the foundations of eternity, a sepulcher of memory carved not from stone but from the compacted dread of a million years. Kaedan descended the spiral staircase with each footfall a muffled percussion against the silence, the torch in his hand guttering as if the air itself refused to witness what lay below. The walls wept a thin, oily secretion that smelled of ozone and old copper, and the temperature dropped with every step until his breath misted before his face in pale, shuddering plumes. He was twenty-two years old, his blood singing with the daily thousandfold amplification of the Awakening, yet his hands trembled with a cold that had nothing to do with the physical chill. His father Joren had pressed the obsidian key into his palm with fingers that shook as though palsied. "You will read what we have all read," Joren had said, his voice a dry, hollow scrape. "You will understand, as I understood, as my father understood, stretching back to Blake himself. And then you will serve, as we have all served, with love and with terror in equal measure."

The key turned in a lock that had not been oiled in ten thousand years, and the adamantine door swung inward on silent hinges. The vault stretched before him, a long, narrow corridor lined with shelves of petrified wood, each bearing rows of bound journals. Some were thick as torsos, others thin as a finger bone. The leather of their bindings was dark with age, and a faint, violet luminescence pulsed from the pages as though the words themselves were alive and breathing. Kaedan stepped forward, his torchlight catching the silver lettering on the spines. *Testament of Thalen, Year 80,000. Testament of Corvin, Year 250,000. Testament of Malachai, Year 600,000. Testament of Draven, Year 400,000. Testament of Harker, Year 900,000.* The names of his ancestors, men whose blood he shared, whose Awakening he had inherited, and whose nightmares he was about to consume.

He reached for the first volume, bound in the hide of something that still twitched when touched. The cover opened with a sound like a ribcage cracking, and the words swam up from the page in a script that was jagged, desperate, the handwriting of a man who had broken his own fingers to keep them steady long enough to write.

---

*I am Thalen, third son of the bloodline, and I write this with the taste of my own vomit still coating my tongue.
