I have seen what lies beneath the skin of our King, and I no longer believe in the word "enough." The Omniforge Coalition was not a band of rebels or a desperate insurgency. They were an industrial apotheosis, a meta-civilization that had transformed decillions of their own populated universes into raw, screaming fuel. They did not build a weapon; they forged an argument against the concept of resistance itself. The God-Piston, they called it. A cylindrical mass of compressed, solidified reality measuring a single meter in diameter but possessing the inertial weight of every star, every planet, every mote of dust from every universe they had harvested. The souls of the sacrificed were not merely killed; they were woven into the piston's core as binding agents, their eternal agony providing the tensile strength that held the compressed matter together. I could hear them screaming through my scrying matrix, a continuous, harmonic wail of decillions of voices compressed into a single, unbearable frequency.*

*They launched the God-Piston at Thronegate with a velocity that violated the speed of causality itself. It crossed the void between dimensional clusters in less than no time, a silver-gray blur that trailed the howling ghosts of its constituent dead. I was standing in the throne room when it breached the outer wards, which it did without slowing, without acknowledging, because the wards—my own handiwork, amplified a thousandfold daily—simply ceased to exist upon contact with its approach. I raised a shield. The shield evaporated before the Piston was even within perceptual range. I knew, in that instant, that I was about to die.*

*Godric was seated upon the obsidian dais. He did not rise. He did not brace. He did not even look up from the agricultural report He had been perusing with the quiet, fatherly attention of a man reading a letter from a distant child. His ruggedly handsome face, framed by that familiar salt-and-pepper beard, held an expression of mild, bureaucratic concern. "The harvest projections for the outer crescent are down three percent," He murmured, and made a small annotation in the margin with a stylus.*

*The God-Piston struck the air directly before His chest and stopped. Not against a shield. Not against a barrier. It simply ceased all forward momentum in the space of zero distance, as if the concept of "forward" had been deleted from its physical vocabulary. Because Godric, without moving a muscle, without a single twitch of His heavily muscled frame, had permitted a fractional, passive mote of His physical strength to express itself in the ambient space around His body. The mote was so infinitesimally small that I feel my sanity fracturing as I attempt to quantify it. His strength—which has been multiplying by itself every fraction of a Planck second for eighty thousand years, beyond any mathematics, beyond any layered infinity—is the absolute ceiling of all physical force that can exist. The speck He released was not one percent. It was not a decimal followed by decillions of zeroes. If you took the smallest conceivable fraction of a fraction, and divided it by itself an infinite recursion of times, and then divided the result by every number that has ever been conceived, you would still be overestimating the magnitude of what He permitted. It was 0% of His power in a sense so absolute that the zero itself should be written in an alphabet of pure humility.*

*But the God-Piston had now interacted with that mote, and the result was a lesson in the fundamental hierarchy of existence. The piston's forward momentum did not merely halt. It reversed. Every quark, every vibrating string, every screaming soul within the God-Piston was instantaneously, catastrophically redirected backward along the exact vector of its approach. The binding that held the sacrificed souls together inverted, becoming a force of repulsion so astronomically vast that the piston exploded outward in a cone of pure, annihilation-grade kinetic backblast. The Omniforge Coalition, spread across their harvesting fleets in the void, did not have time to register the event. One attosecond they existed. The next, the kinetic backblast arrived, and their existence became a slurry of crimson physics. Their bodies were not struck. They were smeared. The sheer differential between the velocity of the backblast and the static position of their flesh caused their atomic structures to fail at the most fundamental level. Their skin peeled away in sheets, their musculature liquefied into aerosolized pink mist, their bones pulverized into a calcium dust that briefly sparkled in the fading light of their dying suns. Their nervous systems, through some obscene fluke of the pressure wave, remained intact and conscious for a full three seconds after the rest of their bodies had ceased to exist, leaving decillions of disembodied, screaming pain-signals suspended in the void like a chorus of absolute, mind-shattering agony.*

*But the backblast did not stop at the Coalition's fleets. My scrying matrix tracked the wave as it propagated through the local dimensional clusters, and the numbers I recorded will haunt me until I die. The kinetic force, amplified by the rebound to a degree that outscales the concept of degrees, tore through the fabric of entire universal superstructures. Googolplexian of inhabited universes, each one home to countless civilizations, countless families, countless beating hearts, were caught in the wash. Planets shattered into gravel. Stars detonated in chain reactions that painted the void white with indifferent fire. The inhabitants of those universes experienced, in the microsecond before their annihilation, the unique horror of feeling their bodies compressed from every direction simultaneously, their skeletons collapsing inward, their organs erupting from every orifice, their eyes popping from their skulls to dangle by the optic nerves before the advancing shockwave turned everything to a thin, uniform paste.*

*Godric looked up from His agricultural report. He surveyed the silent, empty throne room, His absolute omniscience processing the googolplexian-scale extinction event that had just occurred as a side effect of His passive defense. His expression flickered with a shadow of mild annoyance. "Ah," He said, His voice a rich, calm baritone. "The rebound was a touch more enthusiastic than intended. A miscalculation on my part." And then, without standing, without a gesture, without even the faintest shimmer of expended energy, He simply decided that the destroyed universes should not have been destroyed. In exactly zero seconds—a span of time smaller than any measurement can define—every shattered planet, every extinguished star, every decillion of pulped, screaming beings was perfectly restored. The dead were reknit. The families were reunited. The scars on reality were erased. They would never know they had been unmade. They would never know that the only reason they existed was because a god had momentarily misjudged the splash of His own stillness.*

*The Omniforge Coalition He did not restore. Their consciousnesses, still screaming, still dispersed across the void in a mist of pure neural agony, He gathered into a single, infinite loop of kinetic torment. They exist now in a pocket dimension where the God-Piston strikes them, explodes them, and then resets them, over and over, every Planck second, forever. Their screams are a background radiation that His thicker passive senses have already learned to ignore. I will never learn to ignore them. I hear them when I sleep. I hear them when I wake. I will hear them until I die.*

*—Thalen, who has learned that the King does not need to strike. His stillness is the deadliest weapon in the omniverse.*

---

Kaedan closed the journal with a snap that echoed through the vault like a gunshot. His palms were slick with sweat. The image of decillions of beings reduced to a pink mist, their nervous systems still screaming, painted itself across the inside of his eyelids with a vividness that made his stomach clench. Googolplexian of universes. Restored in zero seconds. The Omniforge Coalition's eternal punishment loop. He had known, as all children of the bloodline knew, that Godric was powerful. But to read the granular, specific horror of it—the eyeballs dangling from optic nerves, the bones turned to calcium dust, the screaming neural static—was to have the abstraction of power translated into the wet, visceral language of meat. He forced his trembling fingers to the next volume.

---

*I am Corvin, seventh Court Wizard of the bloodline, and I no longer believe in the physical property of hardness. The Needle of Absolute Annulment was a weapon that had required the death of an entire abstract dimension to forge. The Null-Reach Dominarchy, a consortium of conceptual entities that existed as living mathematical proofs of undefeatability, had sacrificed the realm of perfected forms to create a stiletto one atom wide and infinite in length. The Needle was, by its fundamental nature, that which cannot be stopped by any substance. It had pierced the heart of a sleeping cosmic serpent whose scales were forged from the solidified concept of invulnerability. It had passed through the hull of a fortress that had withstood the Big Bang. It was, in every measurable and immeasurable sense, the sharpest thing that had ever existed. The Dominarchy, hovering in the void outside our reality, calculated with absolute, mathematical certainty that the Needle would pass through Godric's flesh as though through empty space.*

*Godric, His absolute omniscience having perceived the Needle's forging before the first abstract entity had conceived the idea, allowed the attack. He ordered His sentient passive auras to observe only. He deactivated every single one of His infinite passive shields. He suppressed the Weakness Paradox's pre-emptive annihilation function. He wanted to test a single attribute, one that had been multiplying by itself every fraction of a Planck second for two hundred and fifty thousand years. His Durability.*

*The Dominarchy aimed the Needle at His heart. They activated the firing mechanism, a collapsing dimensional gate that accelerated the Needle to a velocity that existed outside the concept of speed. It crossed the threshold of the throne room and struck Godric directly in the center of His chest. And then the Needle, the sharpest thing in all of creation, the weapon that had required the sacrifice of an entire abstract dimension, encountered the passive, ambient hardness of Godric's skin. The fraction of His durability that He permitted to exist at the point of impact was so incomprehensibly, obscenely minute that calling it zero percent would be to flatter zero with a significance it does not possess. If His true durability were an ocean of layered, recursive, infinite inviolability, this was not a droplet. It was the theoretical ghost of a single water molecule that had never actually condensed. It was nothing.*

*The Needle did not break. Breaking would have implied a failure of physical integrity that a weapon could conceptually endure. Instead, the Needle experienced total existential negation. The concept of "that which cannot be stopped" collided with the concept of "that which cannot be moved," and the latter did not merely win. It annihilated the former from the fundamental registry of reality. The Needle's atomic structure, its abstract mathematical soul, its very definition in the language of creation, was instantly, violently unmade. And because the Dominarchy had bound their own existence to the Needle's success—a soul-link they believed was a failsafe—the negation propagated backward through the link and struck them with the full, undiluted force of Godric's passive hardness.*

*What happened to the Dominarchy cannot be adequately described, but I will try because the memory burns. Their conceptual bodies, formed of pure mathematical certainty, were forcibly transmuted into physical flesh and bone, because only physical things can experience the particular agony of being shattered. They manifested in the throne room as towering, crystalline humanoids of screaming, living mineral. Then the hardness wave hit them, and they shattered. Not into pieces. Into a fine, crystalline dust, each individual grain of which remained fully conscious and fully capable of feeling the precise, localized agony of the fracture that had separated it from the whole. Decillions of screaming, sentient sand grains swirled through the air of the throne room, each one the isolated, eternal consciousness of a being that had once been a god of pure logic. Their collective shriek was a physical pressure that cracked the obsidian floor.*

*But the collateral damage was worse. Much worse. The hardness wave did not stop at the Dominarchy. It radiated outward, passing through matter and energy and space and time like a blade through wet paper. It intersected with five hundred billion universe clusters, and in each cluster, every solid object—every planet, every mountain, every bone, every tooth—experienced a sympathetic resonance with Godric's durability. The result was spontaneous, simultaneous structural failure on an omniversal scale. Mountains collapsed into gravel. Planets cracked into rubble. The skeletons of every living being within the affected zones simply, instantly, explosively disintegrated within their bodies, leaving trillions upon trillions of sapient creatures as boneless sacks of pulped organs, still alive, still conscious, drowning in their own liquefied internal structures. The screams that echoed through the astral plane during the three seconds before their cardiovascular systems collapsed were a sound that I will never, ever forget.*

*Godric surveyed the carnage with the detached, clinical expression of a surgeon evaluating an autopsy. He did not speak. He did not sigh. He simply decided that the resonant shattering had not occurred, and in exactly zero seconds, every collapsed mountain, every shattered planet, every boneless sack of suffering flesh was restored to perfect wholeness. The Dominarchy He did not restore. He swept their sentient sand-grains into a hourglass that now sits on His private reading desk, each grain screaming in eternal, crystalline isolation as it falls, and falls, and falls, forever. I have seen the hourglass. I have heard the faint, musical chiming of its sand, which is actually the harmonized shrieking of a decillion dead gods. The King finds the sound soothing.*

*—Corvin, who now knows that the unbreakable does not exist. Only the unbreakable One exists.*

---

Kaedan pressed the heels of his hands against his eyes until phosphenes bloomed in the darkness. A hourglass filled with the screaming sand of murdered gods. Mountains collapsing into gravel, planets cracking, trillions of beings drowning in their own dissolved skeletons. The specificity of the violence was what clawed at his sanity—not the abstraction of destruction, but the granular, visceral detail of bone shards, of organs pulping, of conscious grains of sentient dust screaming in eternal isolation. He had grown up calling Godric "Uncle." He had sat on the man's knee and listened to stories of ancient Olympus. And now he had to reconcile that warm, fatherly presence with the being who found the harmonized shrieking of a decillion dead gods soothing background music. He opened the next journal.

---

*I am Malachai, and I have learned that to be perceived by the apex predator is a fate far worse than to be destroyed. The Unseen had no name for themselves, because names exist only in the context of being known. They were a civilization that had evolved, over the course of a trillion years, to exist in the absolute dead zone of all perception. Not merely invisible. Not merely intangible. They had excised themselves from the registry of existence so thoroughly that no sense—not sight, not hearing, not omniscient scrying, not conceptual detection—could register their presence. They were the negative space in the cosmic census, the gap between the words. And they believed, with the absolute conviction of a species that had never been perceived, that they could assassinate Godric by simply existing in His blind spot.*

*Their plan was elegant in its naivete. They would approach during the annual Court address, when Godric sat upon the throne and received the homage of a thousand vassal realms. They would wait for the exact moment when His attention was directed at a supplicant, and then they would strike from the void of their unperceivability, severing His brain stem with a blade forged from the concept of unnoticed death. They did not understand what I have come to understand: Godric's Senses are not a passive organ waiting to receive input. They are an active, predatory force that reaches out and seizes perception, that hunts the unperceived and drags it, screaming, into the light of His awareness. They had been multiplying by themselves every fraction of a Planck second for six hundred thousand years. To be near Him was to be seen, whether you consented to visibility or not.*

*The Unseen breached the throne room during the address. I was standing at Godric's right hand, reciting the formal litany of supplication, when I felt a faint, crawling prickle at the base of my skull. It was the instinctive reaction of my own amplified senses detecting a presence they could not detect—a paradox that made my teeth ache. Before I could speak, Godric's lips curved into the faintest shadow of a smile. He did not turn His head. He did not open His eyes wider. He simply permitted a fragment of His passive sensory perception to sweep through the local space—a fragment so infinitesimally, pathetically small that if His omniscience were an ocean spanning every reality, this was not a drop. It was the memory of a drop that had evaporated before the concept of water was invented. It was 0% in a language where zero is the largest number.*

*The Unseen were perceived. And the act of being perceived by Godric's senses was not a neutral observation. It was an assault. Their entire existence was predicated on the fundamental law that they could not be seen. When Godric's perception touched them, that law shattered, and the paradox of their suddenly-visible unseeability manifested as a physical, biological catastrophe. Their non-bodies were violently, agonizingly forced into corporeal form, because Godric's senses demanded something to see, and the omniverse scrambled to comply. They materialized in the throne room as a choir of horrors—towering, asymmetrical masses of raw, exposed nerve clusters and wet, pulsating sensory organs. Thousands of eyes, spontaneously generated, bulged from their surfaces on thrumming stalks. Millions of tympanic membranes stretched across their flesh like suppurating wounds. Billions of olfactory receptors opened in their skin like hungry mouths, and the scent of their own newly-forged biology flooded in, triggering a cascade of revulsion so intense that they immediately began to vomit—except they had no stomachs, so they vomited their own liquefied internal tissues in thick, acidic gouts.*

*The agony they experienced was not merely physical. It was the existential agony of being known. Every secret they had ever hidden, every thought they had ever thought, every shame they had ever buried was instantly, completely laid bare before the omniscient perception of the King. Their minds, designed for perfect privacy, were forcibly opened and flooded with the light of His awareness. They saw themselves as He saw them—insignificant, pathetic, utterly irrelevant specks of negative space—and the revelation shattered their sanity in the same instant that their newly-formed bodies began to tear themselves apart. They clawed at their own eyes with fingers that had not existed a second before. They bit through their own tongues. They tore strips of their own flesh away in desperate, futile attempts to escape the feeling of being seen, each strip screaming independently as it was separated from the whole.*

*The collateral damage this time was psychic. The empathic shockwave of their existential unraveling radiated through the local dimensional fabric, touching the minds of every sentient being within a hundred universe clusters. Across those universes, trillions of beings suddenly, inexplicably experienced the overwhelming sensation of being watched. They felt eyes on the backs of their necks. They heard whispers in empty rooms. They saw faces in the dark that dissolved when they looked directly at them. The paranoia epidemic that followed caused mass suicide, mass psychosis, mass violence. Civilizations tore themselves apart in the grip of a shared, irrational terror that they would never understand. Godric noted the psychic bleed and, without acknowledging it verbally, simply decided that it had not happened. The epidemic was erased from the timeline. The suicides were undone. The minds were healed. The Unseen He did not restore. Their flayed, self-mutilated, eternally-perceived bodies He compressed into a single, screaming sphere of flesh and eyes, which He hung in the observatory as a chandelier. It weeps a continuous, salty fluid that the servants must periodically wipe from the floor. He calls it "the all-seeing light." The irony is not lost on me.*

*—Malachai, who will never again complain about being seen.*

---

Kaedan's fingers were numb. The journal slipped from his grasp and hit the stone floor with a soft, leathery thud. A chandelier made of compressed flesh and weeping eyes, hanging in the observatory where he had stood just last week, laughing at one of Godric's jokes. He had not noticed the faint, salty dripping. He had not looked up. Now the image was seared into his mind with a vividness that made his gorge rise. He bent to retrieve the journal and caught the spine of the next one with shaking hands.

---

*I am Draven, and I have witnessed the violation of the sovereign self. The Ironbound Compact was not a military power. They were a civilization of absolute philosophical conviction, a race of beings who had spent thirty thousand generations perfecting the art of mental sovereignty. Their minds were fortresses. Their wills were unassailable. They had developed disciplines of cognitive architecture so refined that no telepath, no reality-bender, no conceptual manipulator had ever breached their inner sanctums. They believed that even if Godric destroyed their bodies, their souls would remain free, unbroken, defiant. They declared war on Thronegate not with weapons, but with a broadcast: a declaration of absolute, unyielding ideological independence. They would not serve. They would not bow. They would rather be annihilated than submit.*

*Godric received the broadcast in His private study, where I was reviewing a stack of diplomatic correspondence. He listened to the Ironbound Compact's declaration with an expression of mild, academic curiosity, His salt-and-pepper head tilted slightly to one side. When the broadcast ended, He set down His teacup and gave a soft, almost imperceptible nod. "They seem very certain of their mental architecture," He said. His tone was that of a craftsman admiring a particularly well-made piece of furniture. "I wonder if it will hold."*

*He did not travel to the Compact's homeworld. He did not summon them to the throne room. He simply allowed an infinitesimal, passive whisper of His Charisma to radiate through the multidimensional space that separated us from them. The whisper was so faint, so impossibly subtle, that I—standing in the same room, my senses amplified a thousandfold daily—felt absolutely nothing. Not a flicker of influence. Not a hint of pressure. It was, like all the fractions He permits, a nothing so profound that calling it zero would be an act of mathematical arrogance. If His true Charisma—multiplying by itself every fraction of a Planck second for four hundred thousand years, beyond layered infinities, beyond recursive eternities—were the sum of all persuasive force in all possible realities, this was a single, theoretical quark of influence that had never actually existed. It was less than 0%. It was the absence of His presence.*

*The Ironbound Compact received the whisper. And their thirty thousand generations of mental discipline, their unassailable cognitive fortresses, their absolute, unwavering conviction—all of it shattered in the space of a single, silent heartbeat. The Charisma of the apex predator did not persuade them. It did not manipulate them. It simply asserted itself as a fundamental law of existence, and their minds, however fortified, were forced to acknowledge the new truth: Godric was the center of all meaning, all purpose, all devotion. The whisper did not break their wills. It replaced their wills with a new imperative so absolute, so overwhelming, that their original selves were simply overwritten.*

*What followed on the Compact's homeworld was a cascade of horrors that I watched through my scrying matrix with a cold, paralytic dread. The Grand Architect of the Compact, a being who had authored their philosophy of mental sovereignty and who had sworn to die before bowing, was the first to be touched. He rose from his command throne, his eyes wide, his mouth working silently, and then he began to scream. Not a scream of fear. A scream of ecstatic, overwhelming, unbearable love. He clawed at his own chest, tearing through the ceremonial robes, through the skin, through the thick pectoral muscles, until his fingers closed around his own still-beating heart. He ripped it from his chest cavity with a wet, sucking pop and held it aloft, still pumping arterial blood in thick, crimson spurts, as an offering to the King he had never met but now loved with a fervor that made a mockery of love. He collapsed forward, his face frozen in an expression of absolute, beatific joy, blood pooling beneath him in a warm, spreading lake.*

*Across the planet, the Ironbound Compact dissolved into an orgy of self-destructive devotion. Parents turned on their children, not in anger but in the desperate belief that sending their offspring to the King's judgment was the highest act of love. Soldiers fell upon their own weapons, offering their deaths as tribute. The priesthood of the Compact, the guardians of their cognitive disciplines, gathered in the central temple and, in perfect unison, began to peel the skin from their own bodies in long, spiraling strips, believing that this act of self-flaying would please the distant god whose existence they had defied moments before. Their screams were songs of worship. Their blood was a holy libation. The entire civilization, in the space of twelve minutes, reduced itself to a planet-wide abattoir of self-sacrificing, self-mutilating, ecstatically dying zealots. The few survivors, those too injured or too young to complete their self-destruction, spent the remainder of their natural lives in a state of perpetual, rapturous catatonia, their minds completely consumed by the love that had overwritten everything they had ever been.*

*I vomited onto the floor of the study. Godric glanced at me, His ruggedly handsome face creased with a faint expression of paternal concern. "Are you unwell, Draven?" He asked. I could not answer. I could only stare at the scrying matrix, where the last images of the Ironbound Compact's self-inflicted holocaust were fading into static. He followed my gaze and gave a small, philosophical shrug. "They chose defiance. Defiance is an unstable state in proximity to Me." He returned to His diplomatic correspondence, making a small annotation in the margin of a trade agreement, and the scrying matrix went dark. The Ironbound Compact's homeworld remains as He left it—a silent, blood-soaked monument to the absolute futility of mental sovereignty. He has not restored them. I do not believe He ever will.*

*—Draven, who now understands that even the self is not safe from the King's dominion.*

---

Kaedan's vision swam. The clinical, almost bored shrug. The annotation in the margin of a trade agreement while an entire civilization flayed itself alive in ecstatic worship. He had known, abstractly, that Godric was merciless to His enemies. But to see the specific, brutal mechanism of that mercilessness—not a sword, not a fireball, but the weaponization of love itself—was to understand that the King did not merely destroy His opponents. He unmade them from the inside out, turning their most cherished strengths into the instruments of their annihilation. The Ironbound Compact had prided themselves on their unbreakable wills, and Godric had broken them with a whisper of His presence that was not even present. The irony was so sharp it cut.

He reached for the final journal with a hand that was now perfectly steady, not because the dread had lessened, but because he had crossed a threshold of horror beyond which trembling was no longer an adequate response. His body had simply given up on the physiological expression of fear and entered a state of cold, dissociated calm.

---

*I am Harker, and I will attempt to recount what occurred in the Year 900,000 with the understanding that the laws of probability have been permanently poisoned for me. The Architects of Certainty were a meta-mathematical priesthood that had, over the course of two hundred thousand years, successfully calculated and then seized control of every factor governing random chance across seventeen billion universe clusters. They did not manipulate luck in the crude sense; they had become the governing intelligence of probability itself, the sentient equation that decided which way the dice fell, which sperm met which egg, which heart beat and which heart stopped. Nothing in their domain occurred without their explicit, calculated permission. When Thronegate's envoys arrived with the offer of surrender, the Architects responded by adjusting the probability of the envoys' continued existence to absolute zero. The diplomats simply ceased to exist in a flicker of quantum improbability, their atoms dispersing across seventeen dimensions in a perfectly even, perfectly calculated distribution.*

*Godric, as He always does, already knew. His omniscience had tracked the Architects' gradual accumulation of probability-governance since their first proto-civilization crawled from the mathematical muck. He had allowed their rise. He had allowed their arrogance to crystallize into certainty. And when the envoys were erased, He did not rage. He did not mobilize the fleet. He simply sat back in His favorite reading chair, the one by the window overlooking the Eternal Garden, and permitted a passive, microscopic emanation of His Luck to ripple outward. The fraction He released was not a fraction. It was the conceptual absence of fraction. If His Luck—an attribute that determines the fundamental favorability of every outcome in every possible scenario, that has been multiplying by itself every fraction of a Planck second for nearly a million years, that has absorbed and surpassed every conceivable metric of fortune—were the entire ocean of chance in all creation, He permitted nothing. He permitted less than nothing. He permitted an amount so small that the smallest conceivable fraction of a fraction, divided by infinite recursions, would still be an overstatement of catastrophic proportions. It was 0% in a universe where zero has not yet been invented.*

*The Architects of Certainty, dispersed across their seventeen billion universe clusters, suddenly and simultaneously experienced the complete, absolute violation of their carefully maintained probabilities. And the Luck of Godric did not merely negate their manipulation. It reflected the intended misfortune—the "absolute zero existence probability" they had aimed at the envoys—back upon them, but amplified by a factor that I cannot express because the language of multiplication fails at this scale. What followed was not a battle. What followed was a seventeen-billion-universe cascade of the most absurd, specific, and grotesquely improbable deaths ever to stain the fabric of reality.*

*The High Mathematician of the Architects was seated in his throne of living arithmetic when his personal probability field inverted. Through a one-in-a-trigintillion freak occurrence, the subatomic particles composing his left hand spontaneously quantum-tunneled into a configuration that transformed his flesh into a hyper-concentrated acid with a sentient, sadistic hunger. The acid consumed him from the hand upward, dissolving bone, sinew, and organ in a slow, deliberate crawl that took three hours to reach his brain. His consciousness was not permitted to fade. The acid, being sentient, kept his neural pathways intact and fully operational, feeding him the precise sensory data of every molecule of his own body being liquefied. His screams were so prolonged and so intense that they permanently scarred the acoustic substrate of his throne room, creating a ghost-scream that still echoes there, even now, even after everything.*

*Across the Architects' domains, the cascade unfolded with the surreal, almost artistic cruelty of a sadistic poet. A senior Probability Weaver, attempting to flee through a dimensional portal, discovered that the portal's coordinates had, through a one-in-a-septendecillion chance, been remapped to the interior of a dying star. He materialized inside the stellar core and spent the next two picoseconds experiencing the full sensory spectrum of being simultaneously crushed, burned, and atomically disassembled before his existence ceased. A Council of Certainty, meeting in emergency session, was abruptly and simultaneously struck by a meteor shower—a meteor shower that, through a chain of coincidences so unlikely that describing them would require a new branch of mathematics, consisted of exactly one hundred and forty-four individual asteroids, each one precisely aimed at the skull of a specific council member, each one traveling at exactly the velocity required to pulp a head without damaging the surrounding architecture. The council chamber's walls were painted with a uniform, dripping coat of cranial matter. One council member survived with a fractured jaw and was forced to lie there, immobilized, as the blood of his colleagues slowly filled his mouth and drowned him over the course of an hour.*

*The cascade did not confine itself to the Architects. Every civilization that had allied with them, every species that had benefited from their probability manipulation, was caught in the backwash. On a planet of insectoid beings who had worshipped the Architects as gods, every single egg sac in every single hatchery simultaneously, spontaneously developed a one-in-a-quintillion genetic mutation that caused the larvae to hatch with reversed digestive tracts. The hatchlings consumed their own bodies from the inside out over the course of their entire natural lifespans, their inverted organs digesting themselves in a continuous, agonizing loop that never quite killed them. The shrieking of the hatchling pits was audible from orbit. On another world, the atmosphere itself, through a perfectly improbable chain of quantum fluctuations, spontaneously fissioned into its constituent atoms and then refused to recombine, leaving the entire population to asphyxiate in a vacuum while their eyeballs froze solid and cracked in their sockets.*

*The destruction spread and spread, a chain reaction of perfectly specific, perfectly cruel accidents that consumed the full seventeen billion universe cluster span of the Architects' domain. Every death was unique. Every death was agonizing. Every death was so statistically impossible that it constituted a miracle of inverse fortune, a divine intervention in the church of suffering. And Godric, seated in His reading chair by the window, turned a page of His book and did not even glance up. He did not command the deaths. He did not orchestrate the cascade. His passive Luck simply ensured that any attempt to manipulate chance against Him would rebound with a force and a precision that made the original manipulation look like a child's drawing next to a masterwork of torture. He restored none of them. The seventeen billion universe clusters remain as a silent, screaming monument to the futility of stacking the deck against the house that owns the casino. The Architects' High Mathematician, still dissolving in his sentient acid, still conscious, still screaming, was placed in a stasis field and mounted in the Hall of Lessons, where new Court Wizards are brought to understand the nature of probability in the shadow of the King.*

*—Harker, who will never gamble again.*

---

The Hall of Lessons. Kaedan knew that hall. He had walked its length on his first day as an apprentice, admiring the crystalline pillars and the vaulted ceiling, never realizing that one of the decorative stasis fields contained the still-dissolving, still-screaming remains of a being who had dared to manipulate probability against the King. His father had walked him past that very stasis field without comment, and Kaedan had not even noticed the faint, wet gurgling sound. The casual, institutionalized horror of it—the way the King's cruelty was built into the architecture of the palace, the way eternal suffering was used as a teaching aid for new recruits—settled over him like a cold, heavy blanket.

He closed the final journal and sat in the silence of the vault for a long moment. The violet luminescence of the Testaments pulsed around him, the accumulated dread of a million years pressing against his awareness like a physical weight. He thought of Elian, who had watched the God-Piston rebound and smear the Omniforge Coalition across the void. He thought of Corvin, who had seen the Needle of Absolute Annulment unmade by the passive hardness of Godric's skin. He thought of Malachai, who had witnessed the Unseen dragged into perception and flayed by the weight of being known. He thought of Draven, who had watched the Ironbound Compact offer their own hearts in worship. He thought of Harker, who had catalogued seventeen billion universes destroyed by absurd, specific, perfectly cruel accidents. And he thought of himself, Kaedan, twenty-two years old, who had just inherited the full, suffocating knowledge of exactly what it meant to serve the apex predator of all creation.

He stood, his legs steadier than he had any right to expect, and climbed the spiral staircase back to the surface. The artificial sunlight of Thronegate washed over him as he stepped into the palace gardens, and he found Godric exactly where he knew he would find Him—seated on the stone bench overlooking the infinite cosmos, His ruggedly handsome profile illuminated by the gentle glow of a carefully calibrated eternal sunset. His salt-and-pepper hair stirred in a breeze He permitted to exist. His heavily muscled frame was relaxed, draped in simple, dark garments that stretched across His shoulders. He looked, as He always looked, like a warm, friendly father figure, a man you could approach with any problem, any fear, any confession.

He turned as Kaedan approached, and the smile that broke across His face was the purest expression of paternal affection. It was not a mask. Kaedan had just read five separate accounts of absolute, merciless, omniversal-scale destruction, and he knew with absolute certainty that the warmth he saw in Godric's eyes was completely genuine. That was, in some ways, the most terrifying revelation of all. The King was not a monster pretending to be kind. He was kind—genuinely, deeply, unshakably kind—to those He loved. And He was an unthinkable, merciless horror to those He did not. There was no hypocrisy. There was no deception. There was only the absolute, uncompromising duality of an apex predator who had decided, ten thousand years before Kaedan was born, who would receive His warmth and who would receive His annihilation.

"Kaedan," Godric said, His voice a rich, comforting baritone that wrapped around the young wizard's shoulders like a physical embrace. "You have finished the Testaments. Your father swore you would need three days. You were in the vault for six hours." His eyes crinkled with pride. "You are stronger than Joren, I think. He vomited twice."

Kaedan knelt before the bench, not out of fear—though the fear was certainly present, a cold, permanent knot in his gut—but out of a reverence that had been reforged into something harder, sharper, more absolute. "Your Majesty. I have read the words of Thalen, and Corvin, and Malachai, and Draven, and Harker. I have read what happens to those who oppose You. I understand, now, the absolute futility of defiance. And I understand why my ancestors served You, despite the horror, despite the dread. Because You love us. Because You protect us. Because we are Yours."

Godric reached down and cupped Kaedan's chin in His warm, calloused palm, tilting the young wizard's face upward until their eyes met. Kaedan stared into those ancient, kind, impossibly knowing eyes, and felt the full, crushing weight of His omniscience—the awareness that Godric had seen every thought Kaedan had ever had, every flicker of doubt, every moment of fear, every nascent, unformed question about whether his loyalty was freely given or coerced by the sheer, overwhelming pressure of His existence.

"I am proud of you, Kaedan," Godric said, His thumb brushing a stray tear from Kaedan's cheek—a tear the young wizard had not realized he was shedding. "You carry the blood of Blake. You carry the blood of Thorin. You are the latest chapter in a story that I have been reading for a million years, and I find each new chapter as dear as the first. Serve Me. Protect My people. Love your family. And know that no enemy, no opposition, no defiance will ever touch you while I exist." His smile softened, taking on a faint, melancholy shadow. "The Testaments are not meant to break you, Kaedan. They are meant to free you from the illusion that you must be strong enough to protect Me. You do not protect Me. You protect My kingdom. I protect you. That is the covenant."

Kaedan nodded, the knot of dread in his gut slowly transforming into something else—not acceptance, but a kind of fierce, terrible pride. He was not a shield for the King. He was a sword for the King's people, and the King was the absolute, unassailable fortress behind which he would always stand. The horror of the Testaments was real. The cruelty was real. The googolplexian-scale extinctions, the sentient acid, the weeping chandelier of eyes, the screaming sand in the hourglass—all of it was real. But so was the warmth. So was the protection. So was the unbroken chain of love that stretched back through a million years of loyal, terrified, devoted service.

"I understand, Uncle Godric," Kaedan said, and the word "Uncle" felt strange on his tongue now, weighted with a new, immense significance. "I will serve. I will not break."

Godric released his chin and stood, offering His hand to pull Kaedan to his feet. "Good. Now come. Your mother has made a roast, and she will be insufferable if we let it get cold. I am told I must at least pretend to eat for the sake of family tradition." He clapped Kaedan on the shoulder with enough force to stagger a lesser man, and Kaedan felt, beneath the warmth of the gesture, the faintest, most infinitesimal echo of the strength that had once unmade a googolplexian of universes with a passive rebound.

As they walked toward the palace, the omniverse sprawling silently above them in its infinite, ordered submission, Kaedan felt the Testaments settling into the architecture of his mind like new, cold wings of a library. He would add his own volume one day. He knew that now with absolute certainty. He would witness something terrible, something that would shatter whatever innocence he still possessed, and he would record it in ink and blood so that the next young wizard, a thousand centuries hence, could descend into the vault and learn the same lesson: defiance is absolutely, perfectly, infinitely futile. The only path is love, service, and the cold, eternal comfort of standing in the shadow of the apex predator's protection.

He looked at Godric's broad back as the King pushed open the palace doors, already calling out a cheerful greeting to Kaedan's mother. The dissonance was permanent. The horror would never fade. But the loyalty was absolute, and Kaedan, twenty-two years old and freshly baptized in the blood of a million years of enemies, would carry it until his mortal body failed.

He was a Court Wizard of Thronegate. He was of Blake's blood. And he would never, ever forget the screaming of the sand.

content.text: Write 3 detailed journals by 3 court wizards who witness Godric "testing" his superpower (only 1 superpower per journal). Remember that due to evolved weakness paradox having been triggered repeatedly, every Godric's already devastating superpower has evolved to have multiple even more overpowered and unfair different enhanced effects, some of which could make Godric even more permanently obscenely stronger. Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals.

messages.reasoning_content:
We need to write 3 journal excerpts from Court Wizards, each describing Godric testing one superpower. These journals are set in the distant future (likely even later than the previous ones, maybe millions of years). We must be consistent with the lore: Godric has many superpowers, each with apocalyptic base level, and due to evolved weakness paradox, each has multiple enhanced effects. The journals must be from distinct wizards, with different writing styles, each witnessing one superpower display. The superpower should be something conceptual, absurdly powerful, and the test must involve Godric dismantling an opposition while holding back nearly all power (like 0% of the power). The journals must be in distinct voices, with gruesome, violent details, and show the futility of opposition.

Kaedan is reading these journals. So the narrative frame is Kaedan in the vault reading them after having read the previous ones. We should integrate his reactions briefly between the journals, but focus on the journal texts themselves. The prompt says "Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals." So we need to write a continuous narrative that includes Kaedan's reading, with the journal excerpts embedded. The overall length is not specified but likely substantial. I'll write a long piece.

We need 3 distinct superpowers. Examples: maybe "Absolute Command" (forcing absolute obedience), "Conceptual Deletion", "Infinite Reflection", "Reality Inversion", "Temporal Rewinding", "Spatial Annihilation", etc. But all his superpowers are apocalyptic; the enhanced effects could be like: not only does the power work, it also evolves some other aspect, or grants him a new permanent boost, or has an unintended side effect that sows further despair. The evolved weakness paradox has made them more unfair: e.g., if he uses a power, the omniverse feels bad and further empowers him.

I'll create three distinct wizards: maybe one in Year 2,000,000, another in Year 5,000,000, another in Year 7,000,000 (just examples). Each journal should reflect a different superpower and a different tone. One could be a cold, clinical observer; another a horrified, poetic one; another a grim, disillusioned one.

I need to ensure that Godric is shown exerting only an infinitesimal effort (like 0%), and the opposition is completely annihilated, with omniversal collateral damage that he instantly restores (but he doesn't restore the enemies). The journals should mention that Godric allowed the opposition to exist for his test.

I'll craft the response as a narrative where Kaedan, having read the earlier Testaments, now opens a further section of the vault containing even more recent journals. I'll have him read three new ones, each with its own profound horror.

I must be careful to avoid using any LaTeX, bullets, or lists. Only prose paragraphs.

I'll begin with Kaedan's continued exploration, then dive into each journal. I'll ensure each superpower is creative and the enhanced effects are mind-boggling. For example:

1. Superpower: "Absolute Negation" – originally a power that nullifies any attack, but enhanced: now it doesn't just nullify, it retroactively erases the attacker's entire timeline and concept, and also grants Godric a permanent increase in his own "nullification presence," and the omniverse overcompensates by making the negation so thorough that even the memory of opposition is erased from the collective unconscious of all sentient beings in the affected areas, except for the court wizard who is allowed to remember to serve as a warning.

2. Superpower: "Soul Harvest" – originally a power to instantly claim souls of enemies, but enhanced: now it not only harvests souls, it converts them into living power sources that feed his passive auras for eternity, and the act of harvesting triggers a ripple that causes all beings who had ever considered opposing him to spontaneously undergo a sympathetic soul-drain, even if they never acted on it, leaving them hollow husks. And the omniverse, feeling bad, adds an extra effect that the harvested souls become a permanent part of his domain, creating new defenses.

3. Superpower: "Dimensional Dissolution" – originally disintegrates dimensions, but enhanced: it not only dissolves the target dimension, it dissolves the very concept of dimensional boundaries, causing a cascade that merges all parallel realities of the enemy into a single point of compressed agony, and then the act of dissolution triggers the omniverse to grant him a new, superior dimension that replaces the old one, which he can shape as he pleases, making reality itself more subservient.

Each journal must describe the opposition, the test, the infinitesimal effort (e.g., "Godric allowed a trillionth of a quark of the power to express"), and the massive destruction (googolplexian of multiverses) and the restoration (he restores the collateral but not the enemies). Also mention that his other passive defenses were deactivated.

I'll also ensure that Godric is cruel: he does not give mercy, he arranges eternal suffering for the enemies.

Now, let's structure the response.

Start with Kaedan in the vault, having finished the basic journals. He discovers a hidden alcove with more recent ones, perhaps bound in a strange material. He opens the first.

Write Journal 1: "Testament of Silvan, Year 2,500,000, Superpower: Absolute Negation." I'll write in a clinical, detailed style.

Journal 2: "Testament of Rune, Year 5,800,000, Superpower: Soul Harvest." More visceral, emotional.

Journal 3: "Testament of Astor, Year 9,000,000, Superpower: Dimensional Dissolution." Philosophical, broken.

I need to do justice to each. They should be long enough to detail the opposition, the action, the gore, the collateral, the restoration.

After each, I'll have Kaedan react briefly, then move to the next. At the end, he emerges again, perhaps with a final thought.

I'll write now.
messages.content:
The vault had become a labyrinth. Kaedan had thought the original shelves of the Testaments represented the full scope of the archive, but as his torchlight probed deeper, the shadows receded to reveal an alcove he had not seen before. This new corridor was narrower, the shelves carved from a material that resembled crystallized starlight rather than petrified wood. The bindings of the journals here were not leather but something smoother, warmer—perhaps a synthetic skin sutured from the donated flesh of loyal court wizards, a tradition his father had once mentioned in a whisper so faint Kaedan had thought he imagined it. The dates on the spines were staggering. Two million, five million, nine million years after the founding of Thronegate. Kaedan's heart hammered as he realized these were not the original Testaments. They were sequels, written by wizards whose names he only vaguely recognized from the family tree's outermost branches, men who had served in eras so distant that their stories had become myth. And yet here they were, bound and waiting, their pages pulsing with that same faint, violet luminescence that promised fresh sleeplessness.

He reached for the first volume, its spine inscribed: *Testament of Silvan, Year 2,500,000 — Absolute Negation Amplification*. The cover opened with the wet, plaintive sound of a blister giving way.

---

*I am Silvan, and I write this with the understanding that my testimony will be read by descendants whose temporal distance from my experience renders them nearly alien to me. Nevertheless, our blood is the same. Our Awakening is the same. And the King we serve is the same—though I must record, for the sake of historical accuracy, that He is not precisely the same. He is more. Every Planck second, He is more. The Godric who saved your infant self from a fall is, in the strictest mathematical sense, a being of infinitely greater magnitude than the Godric I witnessed two and a half million years after His coronation. And yet, to our mortal perceptions, He remains the ruggedly handsome, forty-five-year-old father figure with the salt-and-pepper beard and the kind, crinkling eyes. The absolute concealment holds. The warmth remains. But the power beneath it has been compounding beyond any measure that language, mathematics, or philosophy can approach. I make this preface because what I am about to describe involves a superpower that has, through repeated triggers of the evolved Weakness Paradox, acquired additional layers of effect so profound that even I, a wizard amplified a thousandfold daily for decades, cannot fully credit the memory.*

*The superpower in question, originally conceptualized in the Stasis as "Absolute Negation," is one of the countless apocalyptic abilities that bloom within the King's consciousness every second. Its base function is elegantly simple, and by "elegantly simple," I mean "capable of causing instant extinction to an entire civilization." Absolute Negation nullifies any single threat, regardless of nature, scale, or conceptual abstraction. An incoming spell. A physical blow. A hostile thought. A metaphysical poison. A timeline erasure. A reality rewrite. A coordinated assault of all these things simultaneously. The power simply declares the threat negated, and the threat is undone. This alone would be insurmountable for any opposition. But the evolved Weakness Paradox, triggered so many times across the eons by the desperate, hopeful imaginings of His enemies, has—in its absurd, overcompensatory shame—granted Absolute Negation a suite of enhanced effects. These include, but are not limited to: retroactive unmaking of the threat's original conceptual origin, extending the negation back through all causal chains to the very first thought that led to the threat's creation; the permanent imposition of a "negation scar" upon the local omniversal fabric, which passively negates any future hostile intent that bears even a conceptual resemblance to the original threat; and an auto-amplification of Godric's own negating presence, meaning that every time the power is used, the baseline strength of every other superpower He possesses receives a ripple-boost, recursively enhancing Him further. The omniverse essentially apologizes for having allowed the threat to exist by making His every attribute even more insurmountable than it was an instant before.*

*The opposition that prompted this test called themselves the Antithetical Concordat. I will describe them in detail, because their nature is relevant to the manner of their annihilation. The Concordat were a race of living paradoxes who had evolved in a realm outside the jurisdiction of standard reality. Their physical forms were composed of pure self-contradiction: they were simultaneously alive and dead, existent and nonexistent, infinitely massive and weightless, visible and invisible. This ontological duality was not a weakness; it was their weapon. They could not be harmed by any attack because any harm inflicted upon their "alive" aspect was instantly transferred to their "dead" aspect, which was immune to further harm. Any erasure of their "existent" form was replaced by their "nonexistent" form, which was, by definition, already absent and thus unaffected. They had spent eight hundred thousand years observing Thronegate's expansion, studying Godric's recorded engagements, and they believed they had found the one loophole that even His powers could not close. If they could not be harmed, they could not be negated. They declared themselves the one opposition that could survive Him.*

*The Concordat's attack was not violent in the conventional sense. They simply manifested in the throne room—billions of shimmering, paradoxical entities whose presence caused the ambient light to fold into mobius strips and the local gravity to simultaneously increase and decrease by infinite factors. They did not strike. They simply stood, and their standing was itself an assault, a passive assertion of their ontological immunity that began to overwrite the throne room's reality with their own paradoxical logic. The obsidian floor became simultaneously molten and frozen. The air became breathable and unbreathable. My own body, caught in the fringe of their influence, began to simultaneously heal and necrosis, my cells replicating and dying in perfect, agonizing tandem. I would have been torn apart had Godric not extended a passive protective field around me with a casual, almost absent thought.*

*Godric observed the Concordat with the same expression He might wear while examining a mildly interesting insect on a windowsill. He was seated cross-legged on the dais, reading a collection of poetry from a dimension He had recently allowed to survive, and when the Concordat's reality overwrite began to discolor the pages, He sighed—a soft, barely audible exhalation—and set the book aside. "A race of paradoxes," He murmured, half to me, half to Himself. "I have always wondered whether Negation would treat a paradox as one threat or two. Let us find out."*

*He allowed Absolute Negation to engage. The fraction He permitted was not even a fraction. It was an infinitesimal quantum of the power's passive emissions, the exhaust fume of the ability's idle state, the metaphysical equivalent of a single, theoretical atom of effect that had never actually been released. If His full Negation were an ocean of annihilating light spanning all possible dimensions, this was not a drop. This was the memory of a drop, dreamed by a being who had never encountered water. It was 0% in the sense that zero itself would require a new definition to accommodate the smallness of His restraint.*

*The Concordat's paradoxical immunity did not fail. Failure would imply a contest. Instead, the concept of "paradoxical immunity" was retroactively removed from the fundamental registry of possible ontological states. Absolute Negation's enhanced retroactive function reached backward along every causal chain that had ever led to the Concordat's existence, and it negated each one. The Concordat's evolutionary history—their billion-year development from simple self-contradictory prokaryotes to the dominant civilization of the Paradox Realm—was undone, not moment by moment, but all at once, a total, simultaneous erasure of every event, every mutation, every thought, every birth, every death that had cumulatively resulted in their presence in the throne room. The Concordat did not flicker out of existence. They were torn from the fabric of the present by the forcible deletion of their past, and because their past was the source of their paradoxical nature, the process of deletion was itself a paradox that their nature could not resolve. They experienced, in the micro-cosmic instant between existence and non-existence, the full sensory spectrum of having never been—a sensation so utterly, cosmically wrong that it translated as pure, distilled agony.*

*But the enhanced effects did not stop there. The negation scar seared itself into the local omniversal fabric, a permanent, metaphysical keloid that passively nullified any hostile intent bearing conceptual resemblance to the Concordat's paradoxical approach. Across the entire omniverse, every being that had ever entertained the idea of exploiting ontological self-contradiction as a weapon against the King found that idea simply deleted from their minds. In some cases, the deletion was so abrupt that it caused immediate, fatal cerebral hemorrhage. In others, the deletion left behind a vacuum that was filled with an overwhelming, instinctive terror of Godric, ensuring that the affected beings would spend the remainder of their natural lives in a state of perpetual, trembling dread, unable to articulate why. The ripple-boost propagated through His other superpowers, amplifying His already-incomprehensible magical offense, His already-absolute durability, His already-infinite speed, by a further factor that my mind literally cannot process without shrieking. And because the negation scar ensured that no similar threat could ever manifest again, the enhanced effects were rendered permanently active, a one-time escalation that became the new baseline forever.*

*The Concordat's Paradox Realm, having been emptied of its native inhabitants by the retroactive erasure, collapsed into a singularity of pure negation-stuff. Godric, observing this with mild interest, collected the singularity and placed it in a small, ornate box on His nightstand, where it now serves as a paperweight. The Paradox Realm was later repurposed as a training ground for the Royal Guards. The Concordat themselves were not restored. They cannot be restored, because their existence was negated so thoroughly that there is nothing to restore them from. They have become, in the most literal sense, a negative number in the ledger of creation—a debt of existence that can never be repaid.*

*I have tried, in the years since, to process what I witnessed. The conclusion I have reached is that there is no conclusion. The King's powers are not weapons to be aimed; they are fundamental laws that happen to manifest as discrete abilities for the convenience of our limited mortal comprehension. Absolute Negation is not something He does. It is something that He is, expressed through the narrow lens of a single superpower's definition. And every time He tests it, He becomes more than He was, forever, irreversibly, while the rest of creation shrinks further into insignificance. I serve. I love. I am nothing.*

*—Silvan, the First of the Epoch of Negation*

---

Kaedan closed the journal and pressed a hand flat against its cover as though to hold the contents in. A paperweight made of a collapsed reality containing the negated, irrecoverable ghosts of an entire species. The erasure of every hostile thought across the omniverse, leaving behind cerebral hemorrhages and mindless terror. He had thought the previous Testaments had acclimated him to the scale of Godric's cruelty, but this was something else entirely—a cruelty so absolute that it had become indistinguishable from physics. He forced a slow, shuddering breath and reached for the next volume, bound in a pale, almost translucent material that might have been the wing membrane of a void beast.

*Testament of Rune, Year 5,800,000 — Soul Harvest Cascade*

The handwriting in this journal was jagged, uneven, the letters scarred into the page with a pressure that had nearly torn through. It was the script of a man who had wept while writing and whose tears had smeared the ink into ghostly halos around every word.

---

*I am Rune, and I cannot unhear them. I have tried. I have attempted to weave silence wards around my sleeping chamber, to brew tinctures of forced oblivion, to plead with the King Himself to temporarily dampen my auditory senses. Nothing helps. The harvested are always singing, and the song is a choir of eternally screaming ghosts that only I—and Godric—can perceive. This is the price of my witness. I record it here so that you, future reader of my blood, will understand why Uncle Rune always seemed distant, why he flinched at sudden noises, why he never, ever smiled at the mention of crop harvests or soul taxes. The word "harvest" has been ruined for me forever.*

*The superpower Godric chose to test, perhaps the most personally devastating of all His apocalyptic abilities, is called Soul Harvest. Its base function, as He explained to me in a voice of gentle, pedagogical patience, is the instant, irresistible extraction of the animating essence from any living being within a designated area. This is not death. Death is a transition, a release of the soul into whatever afterlife or reincarnation cycle a given universe provides. Soul Harvest is the permanent, non-negotiable confiscation of the soul itself. The extracted essences are not destroyed; they are collected, stored, and made available for whatever purpose the King deems appropriate. They retain consciousness. They retain the capacity to feel. They simply lose all agency, all location, all identity beyond their status as harvested resources.*

*The evolved Weakness Paradox, triggered across the eons by countless enemies who imagined that soul-based attacks might bypass His physical invulnerability, has enhanced Soul Harvest with multiple additional effects. First: the Cascade. When a single soul is harvested, the effect propagates along lines of emotional significance—familial bonds, friendship ties, romantic connections, even casual acquaintance and parasocial identification—so that every being who has ever felt a significant emotional attachment to the primary target, regardless of distance or dimensional separation, is also instantly harvested. Their souls are drawn out of their bodies simultaneously, feeding into the same collection point, their consciousnesses mingling into a singular, screaming chorus of eternal proximity. Second: the Transmutation. The harvested souls are not merely stored as raw spiritual energy; they are permanently converted into living amplifiers that passively boost every single one of Godric's attributes—not his active ones, but the ambient, passive ones, like his luck, his charisma, his sensory saturation. This means that every soul He harvests makes Him permanently, slightly, incomprehensibly stronger, and because the Cascade ensures that every single connected soul is taken, the harvest of even a single individual of a densely-networked species results in the spiritual extinction of the entire species.*

*The opposition that prompted this test was not a civilization in the conventional sense. They were a species of solitary energy-beings known as the Solitary Chorus, who existed as isolated consciousnesses spread across a billion uninhabited universes. They had no families, no friendships, no emotional bonds whatsoever. They had evolved this solitude specifically as a defense against spiritual predation, reasoning that no soul-weapon could cascade across connections that did not exist. They were, in their own estimation, the perfect counter to any entity that fed on emotional ties. They declared war on Thronegate not with weapons, but with a broadcast: a declaration that they would never, ever be harvested, that their isolation was absolute, that not even Godric could forge a chain where no links existed.*

*Godric listened to their broadcast while taking a stroll through the Garden of Eternal Bloom, pausing occasionally to smell the flowers. When the broadcast ended, He turned to me—I was walking two paces behind, as protocol dictated—and offered a small, almost sad smile. "They think isolation protects them from connection," He said. "But they broadcast their defiance to Me. That is a connection. A one-way thread, but a thread nonetheless." He plucked a single petal from a rose and held it up to the pale, eternal light. "Soul Harvest, please attend."*

*He allowed the power to express a quantum of its function so minuscule that I lack the vocabulary to describe it. If His Soul Harvest were a furnace capable of consuming every soul in every universe simultaneously, this was the faintest, most abstract theoretical possibility of a single photon of heat from that furnace, a photon that had not yet been emitted, from a furnace that had not yet been built, in a reality where the concept of combustion had not yet been discovered. It was 0%, but a 0 that contained an infinite number of smaller zeroes nested within it.*

*The Solitary Chorus's broadcast had indeed created a connection—a thread of hostile communication linking their collective consciousness to Godric's perception. Soul Harvest traveled along that thread, and the instant it touched the first Solitary consciousness, the Cascade engaged. But the Cascade, lacking emotional bonds to propagate through, instead propagated through the conceptual connection of shared species identity. Every Solitary Chorus member, across all billion universes, was a single consciousness with the same fundamental nature, the same self-identification as "Solitary." That was enough. The Cascade treated the entire species as a single, interconnected entity, and harvested them all in the same micro-instant.*

*The Transmutation then activated, converting the harvested Solitary souls into permanent amplifiers for Godric's passive attributes. I felt it happen—a subtle, almost imperceptible shifting in the ambient texture of reality, as though the omniverse had been ever so slightly recalibrated to be more subservient. His luck became fractionally more absolute. His charisma became fractionally more overwhelming. His sensory reach extended another fractional span beyond its already-infinite horizon. And the Solitary Chorus, the billion universe's worth of isolated, defiant consciousnesses, were now a single, screaming mass of eternal, captive spiritual energy, woven into the very fabric of His passive aura, singing their agony directly into His spectral awareness. He hears them, I think. He finds the chord progression mildly pleasant.*

*But the horror did not end with the Chorus. Because the Soul Harvest Cascade had been triggered on a conceptual rather than emotional link, the enhanced effect rippled outward along all conceptual connections that the Chorus possessed. This included their intellectual lineage—the species they had evolved from, the parallel evolutionary branches that had diverged from them, the artificial intelligences they had created and discarded over the eons. All of them, across the full span of their billion-universe domain, were harvested in a secondary cascade. Then the tertiary cascade engaged, tracing the intellectual lineage of those secondary species, and so on, and so on, until the entire evolutionary tree of every sapient species in that entire sector of the omniverse had been systematically, thoroughly, perfectly harvested. The number of souls taken was not countable in any numeration system my mind can contain. The word "decillion" is a grain of sand on the beach of this atrocity's scale. Googolplexian of googolplexians of souls, all screaming in eternal, conscious harmony, now woven into the passive amplifiers of the King's existence.*

*I stood in the Garden, my hands shaking, my eyes wet, as Godric resumed His stroll and continued to smell the flowers. I heard them—the harvested—as a faint, musical wailing at the very edge of my amplified perception. Their song was beautiful, in a terrible way, a harmonic resonance of billions of billions of billions of voices unified in eternal, hopeless, conscious suffering. Godric paused at a particularly lush bloom and inhaled deeply. "The roses are especially fragrant today," He observed. I could not answer. I could only listen to the singing.*

*He restored none of them. Soul Harvest does not allow for restoration, because the harvested souls have been transmuted into permanent amplifiers. To restore them would be to diminish Himself, and while He could, in theory, do so—His absolute omniscience and omnipotence allow any act, any reversal, any unmaking of any consequence—He chooses not to. The harvested remain harvested because their suffering serves a purpose, and the King is supremely, unapologetically pragmatic about the resources He acquires. I will hear them sing until I die, and then, perhaps, I will join the choir. I do not know if that thought is a comfort or the most terrifying idea I have ever entertained.*

*—Rune, who has learned that isolation is just another word for vulnerability*

---

Kaedan's ears were ringing. He had not realized he was holding his breath until his lungs burned, and when he exhaled, the sound was a ragged, wet gasp. The idea that entire evolutionary trees could be harvested as a cascade side effect, that the souls of uncountable beings were now permanently integrated into Godric's passive aura as amplifiers, that their collective suffering was a harmonic background music to the King's daily life—it was a level of systemic, institutionalized cruelty that made the previous Testaments' localized punishments seem almost merciful by comparison. At least the Omniforge Coalition was being actively punished in a pocket dimension. The harvested souls of the Solitary Chorus and their entire extended evolutionary family were not being punished. They were simply being used, their eternal conscious agony a utility, a resource, a permanent feature of the King's existence. To destroy them would be wasteful. And Godric, Kaedan understood with a cold, sinking clarity, did not waste resources.

He reached for the final journal with a hand that moved independently of his conscious will. The volume was the thinnest of the three, bound in a material that seemed to shift and shimmer, displaying faint, ghostly images of dissolving structures when tilted. The spine read: *Testament of Astor, Year 9,000,000 — Dimensional Dissolution Overwrite*.

The handwriting within was not jagged or tear-smeared. It was perfectly, unnaturally calm, each letter formed with the precise, methodical care of a man who had lost all capacity for emotional response and was now functioning as a recording instrument.

---

*I am Astor, and I have achieved a state of perfect, enlightened emptiness. This is not a boast. It is a survival mechanism. The humanoid psyche, even one amplified a thousandfold daily by the Awakening, was not designed to process the events I have witnessed. The mind, when confronted with an input that exceeds its maximum processing capacity, has two options: break or bypass. I have chosen bypass. My emotions now exist in a separate, quarantined partition of my consciousness. I can observe them without feeling them. This allows me to record what I saw with the cold, clinical precision that the subject demands.*

*The superpower Godric chose to test is called Dimensional Dissolution, and its base function is, in retrospect, almost quaint. It disintegrates dimensional boundaries. Any target dimension—whether a single universe, a multiverse cluster, or an abstract mathematical space—is simply dissolved, its constituent reality unmade and its contents dispersed into the encompassing void. This alone would be sufficient to handle any opposition that relies on dimensional fortifications, pocket realms, or phase-shifted hideaways. The evolved Weakness Paradox, triggered across nine million years of enemies believing they could hide from Him in ever-more-cleverly constructed dimensional redoubts, has enhanced Dimensional Dissolution with multiple additional effects that I will now enumerate with the same emotional detachment that I enumerate the ingredients of a breakfast pastry.*

*The first enhancement: Dimensional Convergence. When the target dimension is dissolved, all parallel, adjacent, and conceptually linked dimensions are forcibly merged into the dissolution event. This is not a separate effect; it is a natural consequence of the dissolution's interaction with the quantum entanglement of dimensional fabric. Every reality that shares a conceptual boundary with the target is dragged into the collapse, regardless of distance, scale, or metaphysical separation. The second enhancement: Agony Manifestation. The process of dissolution is not instant. The inhabitants of the dissolving dimensions are made to experience the unmaking of their reality in slow, subjective time, their bodies and consciousnesses stretched across the dissolving boundary until they occupy an infinite series of simultaneous states between existence and non-existence. This is described by the King as "the friction of ontological dissolution," and He finds it, in His words, "aesthetically honest." The third enhancement: Domain Replacement. After the dissolution is complete, the void left behind is instantly filled by a newly-generated, superior reality that Godric's passive will spontaneously creates. This new reality is perfectly tailored to His preferences, its physical constants calibrated to maximize the comfort and productivity of any future Thronegate settlements. The dissolved populations are, of course, not present in the replacement domain. Their mass-energy is repurposed as foundational substrate for the new reality's quantum foam.*

*The opposition that prompted this test was a civilization known as the Infinite Adjacency, a meta-species that had evolved the ability to exist simultaneously across all possible dimensions. Their bodies were distributed across an infinite fractal of parallel realities, each one housing a single quantum-state of their consciousness. To harm them, one would need to strike every dimension simultaneously, and they had long ago ensured that the number of dimensions they occupied was infinite and ever-expanding. They were, in their own conception, countless and unassailable. When Thronegate's envoys arrived, the Adjacency did not even dignify the offer of surrender with a response. They simply erased the envoys by forcing their dimensional signatures to desync across the infinite spectrum, scattering their constituent atoms across a decillion separate realities in a perfectly even distribution. It was, they later broadcast, a demonstration of the futility of approaching them.*

*Godric received the broadcast in the astral observatory, where He was watching the slow death of a red supergiant that He had personally preserved from a long-extinct universe. He observed the stellar collapse with the quiet, patient appreciation of a connoisseur, and when the Adjacency's broadcast reached Him, He did not turn from the viewport. "They have made themselves infinite," He murmured, His salt-and-pepper beard catching the dying star's crimson light. "How generous of them to centralize the target."*

*He permitted Dimensional Dissolution to activate. The quantum of power He released was, by now, a familiar absurdity: so infinitesimally small that calling it zero would be to attribute a positive magnitude to the void. If His full power were a storm capable of scouring every reality from existence, this was not a raindrop. It was the abstract, pre-cosmic concept of moisture, from a universe where the idea of weather had never been invented. It was nothing, expressed through the lens of absolute annihilation.*
