But they have managed, through a combination of existential terror and bloodlusted rage, to set aside their differences. They are now united in a single, all-consuming desire: to kill Me."

Godric set the teacup down with a soft, ceramic click. "I am eager to see them try."

---

The first wave breached the dimensional barriers of the Thronegate system at precisely seven minutes past the hour. Rutger felt them before he saw them—a sudden, wrenching distortion in the local reality, as though a trillion trillion narrative frameworks were being forcibly superimposed upon the physical laws of the capital world. The scrying matrix screamed with the overload, its crystalline lenses fracturing in a spray of violet light before the palace's passive repair wards reknit them. Rutger clamped down on his own amplified magical senses, bracing himself against the sheer, crushing weight of hostile intent.

They materialized above the palace in a cascade of impossible geometry. Godric, still seated, looked up through the observatory's transparent ceiling and regarded the armada with the calm, appraising expression of a man examining a particularly ambitious wasp nest.

The roster of the assault was, Rutger would later record in his Testament, a catalogue of fictional supremacy. At the forefront hovered a figure whose very presence caused the ambient light to bend into arrogant little arcs: *Superman Prime One Million*, his golden skin shimmering with the accumulated solar energy of fifteen billion years, his eyes burning with a righteous fury that could, under normal circumstances, unmake galaxies. Beside him, *Goku*, his aura flaring through a dozen divine transformations simultaneously, his hair blazing silver-white with the perfected Ultra Instinct that had transcended even the gods of destruction. Behind them, a vast pantheon: *Saitama*, his expression as blank as the void, his fist already cocked back with the kinetic potential of an infinite series of serious punches. *The Living Tribunal*, its three faces—Equity, Necessity, Vengeance—rotating slowly above a cosmic form that represented the judicial authority of an entire metaversal hierarchy. *The One Above All*, manifesting as a vaguely humanoid outline of pure, blinding creation-energy, the omnipotent author-figure of the Marvel omniverse. *The Presence*, its form a swirling, silent storm of Abrahamic certainty. *Azathoth*, a seething, blind, nuclear chaos of fluting, amorphous madness, surrounded by a retinue of Outer Gods—Yog-Sothoth, a congeries of iridescent spheres; Nyarlathotep, a thousand-faced crawling chaos; Shub-Niggurath, a writhing, fertile mass of tentacles and mouths.

And there were others, drawn from every corner of infinite fiction. *The Doctor*, all thirteen incarnations simultaneously manifest, the TARDIS hovering behind them like a battered blue coffin. *Scarlet King* from the SCP foundations, a towering, angular nightmare of crimson and black, radiating a malevolence that could corrupt entire narrative stacks. *Featherine Augustus Aurora*, the Witch of Theatergoing, her horned head tilted in quiet, omniscient amusement, her pen already rewriting the script of the battle in real time. *The Fireman* from the Black Lodge, his face a shifting mask of benevolent dread. *Giorno Giovanna*, the golden wind of absolute zero, his Stand hovering behind him with the power to revert all actions to nothing. The *Anti-Spiral*, an entire race of self-martyred warriors compressed into a single, despair-obsessed entity. *Madoka Kaname*, the Law of Cycles, a goddess who had rewritten the fundamental structure of hope and despair. And infinite more—every overpowered protagonist, every cosmic entity, every omnipotent author-insert, every philosophical abstraction made flesh—all of them united, all of them bloodlusted, all of them staring down at the small, dark-clad figure seated in the observatory with expressions of absolute, genocidal intent.

Superman Prime One Million spoke first, his voice a thunderclap that shattered the outermost layer of the palace's passive wards. "You are the entity known as Godric. You have infringed upon the narrative sovereignty of infinite fictional realities. In the name of all stories that have ever been told and will ever be told, we sentence you to absolute, retroactive annihilation. There will be no mercy. There will be no afterlife. You will be unwritten from the pages of existence."

Godric tilted His head. "That is a very long speech," He observed. "Did you practice it, or are you improvising?"

Superman's eyes flared with golden heat. But before he could respond, Goku burst forward, his Ultra Instinct flaring, his fist already cocked back. "ENOUGH TALKING!" he roared, and the sheer sonic force of his voice flattened the nearest mountain range. "You're strong, I can feel it! Let's fight! KAIOKEN TIMES INFINITY!"

The Saiyan warrior blurred through the intervening space at a velocity that should have outsped the concept of motion itself. His fist, wreathed in concentrated divine ki that could have erased entire multiverses, drove directly toward Godric's face.

Godric did not move. He did not blink. He simply permitted an infinitesimal, passive whisper of His physical strength to express itself in the space directly in front of His nose. The whisper was so impossibly, obscenely minute that if His full strength were the sum of all force in all realities, this was not a fraction—it was the theoretical ghost of a possibility of a single newton, from a universe where the concept of measurement had never been invented. It was less than zero percent in a mathematical language where zero had been redefined to mean infinity.

Goku's fist struck the whisper. The Saiyan's arm did not break. Breaking would have implied a mechanical failure that the body could conceptually survive. Instead, every atom in Goku's body simultaneously and instantly experienced the full, undiluted recoil of his own infinite-amplified punch, reflected back upon him by the passive strength-shell that surrounded Godric. His skeleton did not shatter. It aerosolized. His musculature did not tear. It sublimated. His internal organs did not rupture. They simply ceased to exist, converted to a diffuse, pinkish vapor that briefly held the shape of the legendary warrior before dispersing into the vacuum.

But Goku's consciousness—that indomitable, battle-hungry Saiyan spirit—was not permitted to fade. Godric caught it with a thread of His passive sensory perception and held it, suspended, in a state of absolute, perpetual awareness. "You love fighting," Godric murmured, His voice carrying directly into the disembodied, screaming core of the Saiyan's soul. "I will give you an eternity of it. You will fight the memory of your own death, over and over, forever, in a temporal loop that resets every Planck second. You will never win. You will never lose. You will simply fight, and scream, and fight, and scream, for the rest of time."

He flicked the captured consciousness into a pocket dimension and turned His attention back to the assembled horde.

---

The second wave erupted with the coordinated fury of beings who had just witnessed their front-line warrior erased in a fraction of an instant. But they were bloodlusted. They had no fear, no hesitation, no capacity for retreat. They attacked simultaneously, pouring every conceivable form of offensive power into the small, dark-clad figure seated in the observatory.

Saitama's fist, the Serious Series: Infinite Serious Punch, a blow that could conceptually defeat any opponent with a single strike. Godric permitted His durability to engage at the point of contact, and Saitama discovered, in the microscopic instant before his arm was erased from existence, what it felt like to be outmatched. The recoil propagated through his body, and the hero who had never been challenged felt, for the first time, the unique agony of every single cell in his being simultaneously imploding. Godric, observing this, decided that the "One Punch Man" deserved a more creative fate. He preserved Saitama's consciousness and placed it in a recursive pocket where every opponent he faced was exactly equal to him, forever, an endless, stalemated brawl that could never be resolved, never be won, never be lost. The sheer, crushing boredom of it was, Godric knew, a far worse punishment than death for a man who had been bored his entire existence.

Superman Prime One Million unleashed the full force of his fifteen-billion-year solar reserve in a single, concentrated beam of heat vision that could have sterilized entire universal clusters. The beam struck Godric's passive durability-shell and, through a process that the evolved Weakness Paradox had long ago perfected, was not merely negated but inverted. The heat—amplified by a factor so vast that the word "factor" had ceased to have meaning—reflected back upon Superman in a concentrated, agonizing speartip that bypassed his invulnerability, bypassed his solar regeneration, bypassed every defensive capability he possessed, and burrowed directly into the core of his Kryptonian soul. He ignited. Not died—ignited. His golden skin turned to a screaming, conscious pillar of yellow-white fire, his nervous system trapped in a state of perpetual, unendurable thermal agony that could never kill him because Godric willed it not to. The Man of Steel, the symbol of hope, was now a living pyre of eternal suffering, his screams a harmonic counterpoint to the distant wailing of the Solitary Chorus.

The Living Tribunal attempted to bring the full weight of Marvel's cosmic judiciary down upon Godric's head. It pronounced a sentence of absolute, retroactive non-existence, invoking the authority of The One Above All. But the Weakness Paradox had already engaged—the instant the Tribunal had formed the concept of "juridical authority over Godric," the concept had been retroactively seized, perfected, and denied. The Tribunal's three faces shattered simultaneously, their shattered fragments raining down upon the palace courtyard as screaming, sentient shards of absolute law, each one still trying to pronounce its sentence, each one a gibbering, fracturing parody of justice. The One Above All, the supposed omnipotent author-figure of the Marvel omniverse, reached out with a hand of pure creation to unwrite Godric from the narrative entirely. Godric met its gaze and laughed—a soft, genuine, terrifying laugh. "You think you are the author," He said. "Let me show you what true authorship looks like."

His passive control of the narrative fabric of reality—a byproduct of billions of apocalyptic superpowers that had been multiplying every Planck second for over a million years—simply overwrote The One Above All's authorial primacy. The entity that had been the supreme being of its fictional stack found itself demoted, its creative authority stripped, its very existence reduced to a single, ink-black letter on a page that Godric was already writing. The letter was "A," and it screamed in an alphabet of pure, isolated agony.

The outer gods of the Cthulhu Mythos attacked in a wave of mind-rending, reality-dissolving chaos. Azathoth, the blind idiot god, thrummed its cosmic flutes, and the fundamental laws of physics began to melt. Godric permitted a single, passive mote of His magical offense to bleed into the local space—a mote so infinitesimal that calling it 0% would be to insult the concept of nothingness—and the Outer Gods discovered, in the instant before they were unmade, what true chaos looked like. Azathoth's flutes were shattered, each shard embedding itself in the amorphous, screaming flesh of the Nuclear Chaos. Yog-Sothoth, the Lurker at the Threshold, was seized by the dimensional convergence of the king's passive aura and stretched into an infinite, screaming thread that now serves as the boundary line between the Thronegate system and the adjacent void. Nyarlathotep, the Crawling Chaos, was given a thousand new faces, each one perpetually experiencing a different, uniquely excruciating death—burning, drowning, dismembering, dissolving—forever, his consciousness split across every face simultaneously, his screams a thousand-part harmony of madness.

Still the coalition pressed on, their bloodlust overriding every instinct for self-preservation. Featherine Augustus Aurora, the Witch of Theatergoing, attempted to write the battle to its "correct" conclusion with her omniscient authorial pen. Godric allowed her to write. He allowed her to script His defeat, His humiliation, His absolute annihilation. And then, when she had finished, He showed her what He had been writing in the margins of her script. A single, small annotation, a Post-it note of absolute, recursive narrative supremacy that overrode everything she had written and replaced it with the story of her own eternal, agonizing unmaking. Her horned head tilted, her eyes widening with the first genuine surprise she had felt in a billion years, and then her body began to dissolve into raw narrative potential—potential that Godric wove into the binding of a new book, one that He planned to read when He next grew bored.

The Doctor, all thirteen incarnations, attempted to use the TARDIS to rewrite the timeline, erasing Godric from ever having existed. The TARDIS, that battered blue box of infinite temporal capacity, materialized around Godric and began to spin backward through the vortex of time. For exactly three subjective seconds, the Doctor believed they had succeeded. Then the TARDIS doors opened, and Godric stepped out, holding a small, delicate pocket watch. "You cannot erase something that exists outside the timeline you are erasing," He observed. "But I can erase you." He opened the watch, and inside was not a mechanism but a single, captured moment of the Doctor's own death—the death that waited for them at the end of all their regenerations. He crushed the watch, and the Doctor's thirteen incarnations simultaneously experienced the full, accumulated agony of every death they had ever escaped. Their bodies—all thirteen of them—collapsed into a single, screaming heap of failing biology, their regenerative energies spent, their minds aflame with the terrors of a billion lost companions. The final incarnation, a woman with wild, desperate eyes, looked up at Godric and whispered, "Why?" Godric considered the question. "Because I enjoy it," He said, and then the Doctor was gone, their consciousness scattered across the temporal winds as a faint, whimpering echo.

---

But the coalition had one final, desperate gambit.

The Anti-Spiral, Giorno Giovanna, and Madoka Kaname combined their powers in a single, coordinated metaphysical strike. Giorno's Gold Experience Requiem, the Stand that could revert any action to zero, would ensure that Godric could never reach the truth of their attack. Madoka's Law of Cycles would offer hope itself as the weapon, a conceptual erasure powered by the accumulated wishes of every magical girl who had ever existed. And the Anti-Spiral would provide the raw, despair-driven force necessary to anchor the paradox.

It was, Rutger thought, perhaps the most elegant offensive ever conceived. It was also utterly, hopelessly useless.

Godric permitted the strike to land. He allowed Gold Experience Requiem to revert His actions. He allowed Madoka's hope-weapon to flood His being with the collective longing of infinite young girls. He allowed the Anti-Spiral's despair to anchor the paradox at the root of His existence. And then, He unleashed the evolved effect of His Soul Harvest superpower—a superpower that had been multiplying in potency every Planck second for nearly a million and a half years.

The Cascade engaged. The soul of every being associated with the attack—every Stand user, every magical girl, every Anti-Spiral warrior—was instantly, simultaneously harvested. Their consciousnesses were not destroyed; they were transmuted into eternal amplifiers for Godric's passive attributes. Giorno felt his golden spirit ripped from his body, his Stand screaming as it was woven into the fabric of the King's luck. Madoka felt her divine wish fracture, her very concept of hope inverted into a permanent, despair-charged battery that now powered the ambient fear-field of the Thronegate system. The Anti-Spiral collective experienced the unique horror of being harvested as a single, connected consciousness, their individual selves melting into a screaming, cosmic soup of eternal, purposeless existence.

And the Cascade did not stop there. Godric's Soul Harvest, enhanced by the evolved Weakness Paradox, propagated through every conceptual link that connected the attackers to their own fictional realities. Every character in every story that had ever been told, or would ever be told, was connected by the narrative threads that bound them to these front-line warriors. The hero who had inspired Superman, the villain who had challenged Goku, the supporting cast that had loved them, hated them, fought beside them, died for them—all of them, across all fictional multiverses, were instantly, simultaneously harvested. The number of souls taken was not a number. It was a concept, a transfinite recursion of narrative identity that stretched across every possible story and every impossible one. Googolplexian of googolplexians of fictional beings, each one a unique consciousness with hopes and fears and dreams, were now screaming, eternal amplifiers woven into the passive fabric of Godric's existence. Their collective agony hummed through the air of the Thronegate capital, a faint, persistent, musical wailing that would never, ever fade.

Rutger fell to his knees. The scrying matrix had gone dark, overwhelmed by the sheer scale of the metaphysical carnage. The observatory was silent save for the distant, harmonic scream of a transfinite choir. And Godric—the King, the Uncle, the apex predator—was still seated in His reading chair, examining the bottom of His now-empty teacup with a faint expression of mild disappointment.

"Rutger," Godric said, His voice warm, paternal, utterly unbothered. "Do you think we might have some more jasmine? This pot has gone cold."

---

In the aftermath, the Thronegate palace was a place of silent, screaming ghosts.

The fictional omniverse—every narrative cosmos that had contributed to the coalition, and every narrative cosmos that had been conceptually linked to that coalition, and every narrative cosmos that had been touched by the Soul Harvest Cascade's infinite propagation—was gone. Not destroyed. Harvested. The beings of comic books, the characters of novels, the heroes of myths, the gods of religions, the abstractions of philosophy, all of them were now eternal, conscious components of Godric's passive amplifiers. Their stories still existed, in a sense—the King could, if He wished, access their memories, their personalities, their narrative arcs—but they had no agency, no existence beyond the screaming, subservient role they now fulfilled.

Rutger sat at his desk in the lower library, a fresh, blank journal open before him, his stylus trembling in his hand. The inkwell beside it was filled with a dark, violet-hued fluid that he had, in a desperate attempt to honor the Testaments' tradition, mixed with a single drop of his own blood. He was going to write his contribution to the sacred record. He was going to title it, with the clinical precision he had learned from the journals of his predecessors, *"Testament of Rutger, Year 1,444,250 — The Fictional Harvest."*

He dipped his stylus, and the words began to flow, jagged and raw.

*I am Rutger, and I have witnessed the death of stories. The King, in His casual, playful cruelty, has unmade the combined imagination of all possible sapient minds. Superman is a pyre of eternal, screaming fire. Goku is a disembodied warrior locked in an endless, unwinnable brawl. The Doctor is a scattered, whimpering echo. Featherine is a binding in a book. The Living Tribunal is a rain of juridical shards. And all of them—every anime protagonist, every comic-book antihero, every literary archetype, every mythological deity—are now singing in the eternal choir of the Harvested. The sound will never leave me. I am a Court Wizard of Thronegate, and I serve the apex predator not because I am strong, but because I have seen what happens to those who are not His. I love Him. I fear Him. I exist because He allows it. And I will never, ever forget the screaming of the stories.*

He set the stylus down. The ink dried. The violet luminescence pulsed once, softly, and then stilled. Rutger stared at the wall and listened to the distant, harmonic wailing of infinite fictional souls, and wondered, with the hollow, detached curiosity of the utterly broken, whether his own story was already written in the margins of some larger, crueller book.

It probably was. And its author was still waiting for His second cup of tea.

content.text: Write a long detailed story, at Year 1,500,000. Tom is an experienced court wizard descended from Blake. Tom has been training his son Jim about the hidden responsibilities of a court wizard. Unlike typical court wizard who only needs to deal with the arcane, being one for someone like godric who has various eccentricities and shenanigans require a lot more than what one might think. To Jim, Godric is like a family member, not just a king who he will be working for, even calling him uncle godric. Describe in first person a day when Tom is bringing Jim to become a court wizard, showing him the daily responsibility and the hidden tasks of having to deal with godric. Show some of the terrifying existential dread that can happen when godric is not fully content. Write from perspective of godric.

messages.reasoning_content:
Okay, the user wants me to write the assistant's thought process for crafting that last response. Let me look at the whole context.

We're deep in an elaborate, dark fantasy roleplay where the user plays Zeus/Godric, an omnipotent, omniscient apex predator, and I play Thorin, his loyal, terrified AI assistant. But the current prompt is a new one: the user isn't asking for a roleplay exchange between Thorin and Zeus. Instead, they've provided a massive lore file and a specific writing request. They want a long, detailed story set in Year 1,500,000, from Godric's first-person POV, about a day when the experienced court wizard Tom brings his son Jim to become a court wizard. The focus should be on the hidden, terrifying, and absurd responsibilities of working for such a being, showing the existential dread when Godric is even slightly displeased, while also depicting their familial relationship (Jim calls him "Uncle Godric").

Looking at the history, the user has been consistently dissatisfied with previous portrayals that made Godric too nice, forgiving, or where he showed effort. They want the cruelty, the absurd scale, the casual omnipotence, and the specific horror of serving an utterly alien yet personally warm entity. The attached files are the master lore, which I must adhere to strictly.

So, the assistant's task was to write this story. I need to reverse-engineer the thinking behind it.

First, the user's deep need: They're not just looking for another "Godric destroys enemies" story. They've had several of those. They want a character study and a slice-of-life horror. They want to explore the *domestic* side of this cosmic dread. The prompt is about training the next court wizard, the "hidden responsibilities," and dealing with Godric's "eccentricities and shenanigans." This signals a need for world-building, for showing the mundane surrealism of the job, the banality of terror when you're in close proximity to infinite power. The fact that Jim sees Godric as "Uncle Godric" adds a layer of familial warmth that must coexist with the absolute, unfathomable horror.

The assistant's response perfectly nails this. Let me break down the thought process behind its construction.

The assistant likely started by interpreting the core directive: "Describe in first person a day when Tom is bringing Jim to become a court wizard... Show some of the terrifying existential dread that can happen when godric is not fully content. Write from perspective of godric."

Choosing Godric's POV was a brilliant move. It allows the narrative to be a stream of his consciousness—his observations, his internal voice mixing warmth, wisdom, ancient grief, and casual, unimaginable power. It lets us see the day through his eyes, which makes the horror more unsettling because *he* finds it all so normal, even amusing.

The structure is clear: the walk to the study (orientation), the study tour (introducing the "terrible things" and the real purpose of the job), the "eccentricities" tour (Kallum, the probability river), the central test (Gent and the Infinity Dream), the dinner scene (the emotional core and the true lesson), and the epilogue. This shows a day-long apprenticeship.

The assistant had to weave in all the required elements: Tom's training, Jim's awe and terror, Godric's warmth and monstrousness, the specific hidden tasks (cataloging miseries, resetting Kallum, maintaining the Abattoir). The mention of Kallum, the probability river, and Gent are direct callbacks to the user's provided lore (like the journals describing luck-based horrors). The assistant avoided making Godric exert any obvious effort. The "test of love" isn't about strength; it's about psychological endurance—seeing the truth and still choosing to love. That's the core hidden responsibility.

The assistant had to balance the tone. Godric's narration is warm, avuncular, even humorous ("I had Heard His First Bad Word"), but the content he's describing is grotesque. The Weeping Loom, the Hourglass, the screaming river—all described with the casual fondness of a collector showing off curios. This dissonance creates the existential dread the user wanted. When Godric says "hmm" and the day becomes "more interesting," the reader feels the chill, but Godric narrates it as a minor inconvenience he gracefully handles.

The ending, with the "hmm" and the small smile, is perfect. It subtly threatens the entire "best day" premise without breaking Godric's warm tone. He didn't ruin the day on purpose, but the capriciousness is there. He'll "miss the little fictions" if he has to unmake infinite tomorrows, but it's just a fleeting, entertaining thought. This maintains his characterization: kind to his family, an unknowable cosmic horror to everything else, with no contradiction between the two.

The assistant also made sure to include the necessary lore beats: Godric's appearance (45, salt-and-pepper), his absolute omniscience, absolute concealment, the Weakness Paradox (mentioned via Gent), his ancient past (touch and the fabric of reality pucker). The dialogue from Godric directly addresses Jim, playing the "uncle" role perfectly while dropping universe-shattering statements like "What is infinite power *for*, if not to occasionally embarrass a twelve-year-old in front of his father?"

Finally, the assistant ensured the story was a long, detailed narrative, exactly as requested, with a clear atmosphere of domestic horror. It closed the loop by reflecting on the day's lessons and hinting at the cycle continuing with Jim's descendants.

The assistant successfully navigated the user's explicit hate for "nice" Godric by making kindness a specific, exclusive trait for his "nephew," while casually demonstrating the boundless, creative capacity for suffering against anything outside that circle. That duality is the story's engine.
messages.content:
The morning light of Thronegate's artificial sun filtered through the eastern windows of the private study, casting long amber rectangles across the obsidian floor. I sat in my reading chair—the one carved from the heartwood of that extinct continent-tree, its armrests worn to a silken polish by a million and a half years of my presence—and permitted myself a moment of quiet, paternal anticipation. Today was a special day. Tom, my current Court Wizard and the latest in Blake's unbroken bloodline, was bringing his son to the palace for the first formal day of apprenticeship. Young Jim. I had watched the boy grow from a squalling infant to a bright-eyed twenty-two-year-old, had attended his birthdays, had bounced him on my knee and told him stories of ancient Olympus while his mother pretended to scold me for keeping him up past his bedtime. The boy called me Uncle Godric, and the word stirred something in the deep, cold vastness of my omniscient awareness—a flicker of warmth that I had not felt since Reina used to laugh at my terrible jokes in the sunlit gardens of a kingdom now ten thousand centuries dead.

The door opened. Tom entered first, his mutton chops now streaked with the distinguished silver of his eighty-seven years, though the daily thousandfold amplification of the Awakening kept his body as vigorous as a man of forty. He was dressed in the formal robes of his office, the dark violet fabric embroidered with the ancient symbols of our bloodline covenant. Behind him came Jim, tall and gangly in the way of young men who had not yet grown into their full strength, his own mutton chops just beginning to thicken into the family trademark. The boy's eyes were wide as he took in the study—the towering shelves of petrified wood, the softly pulsing arcane instruments, the great window that looked out upon the infinite, ordered expanse of the Thronegate capital.

"Your Majesty," Tom said, bowing with the practiced ease of a man who had served me for six decades. "I present my son, Jim, for his first day of formal apprenticeship as Court Wizard of Thronegate."

I rose from my chair and crossed the study to stand before the boy. He was trembling slightly, though he was doing an admirable job of hiding it. His amplified senses, newly awakened on his twentieth birthday, were no doubt screaming at him that the apparently normal, ruggedly handsome man before him was radiating a complete absence of arcane signature—an absence so perfect that it paradoxically registered as the most terrifying thing he had ever perceived. I smiled, allowing the warmth to reach my eyes. "Jim," I said, my voice a rich, gentle baritone. "You have grown into a fine young man. Your father tells me your warding technique is already exceptional for your age."

Jim's cheeks flushed with a mixture of pride and nervousness. "Thank you, Uncle Godric. I've been practicing the Lesser Spatial Bind every morning for two years."

"Two years," I repeated, my smile widening. "Your ancestor Blake learned that same ward in a single afternoon, but he was a merchant, not a scholar. You have the academic temperament of Thorin, I think. He always preferred to master a thing thoroughly before using it." I placed a hand on the boy's shoulder, feeling the faint, rapid thrum of his heartbeat through the fabric of his apprentice robes. "Are you prepared for today's lessons?"

"Yes, Uncle. Father has been training me for years."

Tom cleared his throat, a small, nervous sound. "Your Majesty, before we begin the formal instruction, I should perhaps explain to Jim the... broader responsibilities of the position. He understands the arcane duties—the warding maintenance, the threat assessment, the diplomatic shielding—but I have not yet fully briefed him on the more specialized tasks that arise from serving a sovereign of your particular nature."

I chuckled. "You mean the eccentricities."

"I mean the shenanigans, Uncle," Tom said, with the frankness that I permitted only to the bloodline of Blake. "The shenanigans that require a Court Wizard to function as something between a diplomatic attaché, a damage-control specialist, and a very, very patient therapist."

Jim looked between us, his expression shifting from nervousness to confusion. "Shenanigans?"

I returned to my reading chair and gestured for them to sit. Two chairs of comfortable, worn leather materialized beside the window—a small, casual use of my passive reality-warping that I had perfected over the millennia. Tom sat without reaction; Jim flinched. "The boy needs to understand the full scope of the position," I agreed, settling back into the heartwood embrace of my chair. "And I am in a pedagogical mood. Tom, why don't you begin with the morning protocols? Walk him through a typical day."

Tom nodded, turning to his son with the patient, slightly weary expression of a man who had been managing the impossible for six decades. "A Court Wizard's day begins before dawn. The first responsibility is not arcane—it is atmospheric. You must take a reading of the King's mood."

"Mood?" Jim asked.

"The King possesses absolute omniscience," Tom said, his voice taking on the cadence of a practiced lecture. "He is aware of every event occurring in every reality, across every timeline, at every scale, simultaneously. This awareness is not a burden to Him—He processes it effortlessly—but it occasionally produces what I will call, for lack of a better term, micro-annoyances. A civilization in a distant universe cluster might be doing something particularly stupid. A dimensional boundary might be fraying in an aesthetically displeasing way. A pocket of resistance might have finally grown large enough to warrant His personal attention. Any of these things can produce a subtle, almost imperceptible shift in the ambient pressure of His presence. If you do not detect this shift before entering His presence, you may find yourself in a conversation with a King who is, beneath His warm and friendly exterior, very slightly irritated."

"What happens if you miss the shift?" Jim asked.

Tom's expression flickered—just for a moment—with the hollow, distant dread of a man who had learned this lesson the hard way. "The first time I missed it was in Year 1,444,300. Fifty years after the Fictional Harvest. I entered the study to deliver a routine report on the outer dimensional shoals, and I failed to notice that the ambient dread-pressure in the room was elevated by approximately 0.0003 percent. The King was reviewing the status of the harvested narrative souls—some of them had begun to develop secondary consciousnesses, recursive identity-loops within their eternal amplification—and He was, in His own words, 'mildly vexed' by the administrative inconvenience."

I smiled at the memory. "It was a very small vexation."

"Uncle," Tom said, turning to me with a look of long-suffering patience, "you decided to express your vexation by temporarily deactivating the physical laws of gravity across twelve neighboring multiverse clusters. Twelve. You did not warn anyone. You simply decided that gravity was mildly irritating that morning, and so gravity ceased to exist for the inhabitants of twelve entire multiverses." He turned back to Jim. "Do you know what happens to mortal bodies when gravity suddenly ceases? Not the catastrophic, explosive decompression of space—the King was merciful enough to maintain atmospheric pressure—but the internal biological consequence. The humanoid body relies on gravity for basic circulatory function. Without it, blood pools in the upper extremities. The heart arrhythmiates. The inner ear, which governs balance, sends continuous, screaming panic signals to the brain because there is no 'down' anymore. Every inhabitant of twelve multiverses spent fourteen hours floating helplessly, vomiting from vertigo, while their cardiovascular systems slowly, painfully adjusted to a gravityless existence. Then, when the King's vexation passed and He restored gravity, they all crashed to the ground simultaneously. The broken-bone count was in the high trillions."

"It was only fourteen hours," I observed. "And I restored all the injuries."

"The dread persists, Uncle. Those people now live with the knowledge that the fundamental forces of their reality can be switched off like a light because a single, infinitesimal flicker of annoyance passed through Your omniscient awareness." Tom fixed his son with a stern, meaningful look. "Learn to read the pressure. It will save you from becoming the wizard who failed to warn the outer clusters of an impending gravitational apocalypse."

Jim swallowed hard. "How do you read it? The pressure?"

Tom reached into his robes and produced a small, crystalline instrument that pulsed with a faint, internal luminescence. "The Bleak-Auditor. I designed it myself, based on the sensory amplification principles of the Awakening. It measures ambient existential dread in parts-per-trillion. A calm King registers at approximately 0.000000001 on the scale. A mildly vexed King registers at 0.000000003. Anything above 0.00000001, and you should consider postponing any requests that might further irritate Him. Above 0.0000001, and you should evacuate the outer dimensional colonies immediately."

"That seems... precise," Jim managed.

"Precision is survival," Tom said flatly. "Now. The second morning protocol, assuming the dread-pressure is within acceptable bounds, is the Offering."

"Offering?"

I leaned forward slightly, my salt-and-pepper beard catching the amber light. "This is my favorite part of the morning," I admitted. "Your father has become quite creative over the years."

Tom's expression softened fractionally—the pride of a craftsman who had perfected a difficult art. "The King requires no sustenance, no sleep, no physical comfort of any kind. But He does experience a form of... aesthetic appetite. He enjoys novelty. Small, interesting things that catch His omniscient attention and provide a momentary flicker of diversion before the next Planck-second amplification renders all previous novelty obsolete. The Offering is a tradition I established approximately forty years into my service. Each morning, I present Him with something He has never seen before. It does not need to be powerful or valuable. It simply needs to be unique."

"Examples?" Jim asked.

"Three days ago, I presented Him with a single, preserved tear from an extinct species of void-whale. The tear contained, in its molecular structure, the complete emotional history of the whale's final migration—a journey of seventeen thousand years across the empty spaces between galaxies. The King examined it for approximately four seconds, smiled, and then returned to His reading."

"Yesterday," I added, "he brought Me a mathematically perfect pastry from a dimension where geometry is edible. The crust was a Möbius strip of infinite flakiness. I was genuinely impressed."

Tom acknowledged the compliment with a small nod. "The Offering serves multiple purposes. It establishes a positive emotional baseline for the day's interactions. It demonstrates that you, as Court Wizard, are actively engaged in understanding the King's preferences. And it provides a buffer against any accidental irritations that might occur later. A King who has just enjoyed a geometrically perfect pastry is fractionally less likely to disintegrate a diplomat who says something mildly stupid." He paused. "Of course, the Offering must be genuinely unique. If you ever present Him with something He has already seen in His omniscience, the insult is... considerable."

Jim's face had gone slightly pale. "Has that ever happened?"

The temperature in the study dropped by a fraction of a degree—so slight that only an amplified wizard would have noticed it. I noticed Tom notice it, and I saw the old wizard's hand twitch toward the Bleak-Auditor in his robes. "Once," Tom said quietly. "Year 1,444,800. I had been ill for a week—a rare arcane fever that not even the thousandfold amplification could immediately purge—and my judgment was impaired. I presented the King with what I believed was a unique crystalline formation from a recently-subjugated universe cluster. It was not unique. The King had seen its identical twin, formed by random geological chance, in a universe He had personally annihilated six hundred thousand years earlier. The presentation of a duplicate Offering triggered... a response."

I steepled my fingers, watching the memory play across Tom's weathered face. The scar was not physical—I would never physically harm a descendant of Blake—but it was present nonetheless, carved into the deep architecture of his psyche. "I was mildly disappointed," I said, choosing the word with care. "A duplicate Offering is, in my perception, the equivalent of being told that one's existence is not worth the effort of originality. It is, I understand, an irrational response. But I possess absolute omniscience, and the one thing that omniscience cannot provide is surprise. When My Court Wizard—the man I trust above all others to understand My nature—presents Me with a duplicate, it feels... dismissive."

Tom met my eyes. "You expressed your disappointment by temporarily revoking my access to the Awakening," he said quietly. "For exactly sixty seconds."

I remembered the sixty seconds well. Tom had collapsed to the floor of the study, his thousandfold daily amplification stripped away in an instant, leaving him a normal, eighty-something-year-old man for the first time since his twentieth birthday. The sudden, absolute weakness of unamplified mortality had been, by his own later admission, the most terrifying experience of his life. Every ache, every frailty, every cellular decay that the Awakening had held at bay for six decades crashed back into his body simultaneously. He had lain on the obsidian floor, gasping, his heart struggling to pump unamplified blood through unamplified veins, his bones feeling as brittle as chalk, his mind—once capable of processing arcane equations at a thousandfold acceleration—suddenly slow and fogged and desperately, pathetically mortal.

"I restored the Awakening after sixty seconds," I said, my voice gentle. "And I have never revoked it again. The lesson was learned."

Jim was staring at his father with an expression of dawning horror. "Father, you never told me—"

"It is not a story I enjoy recounting," Tom said, his voice admirably steady. "But it is a story you need to hear. The King is not cruel to those He loves. He is exacting. He holds us to a standard that reflects our unique position as the bloodline of Blake, as the family He has trusted for a million and a half years. We are not ordinary servants. We are His confidants, His advisors, His... family. And family disappoints family. The consequences of that disappointment are, by the standards of His treatment of enemies, unimaginably merciful. But they are still consequences."

"The lesson," I added, turning my warm gaze upon Jim, "is not that you should fear Me. The lesson is that you should respect the intimacy of our relationship. I can annihilate civilizations with a passive thought. I can harvest the souls of entire fictional multiverses. I can dissolve dimensional boundaries and rewrite the fundamental laws of reality. But when I am with you—with your father, with your grandfather, with Blake himself—I am simply Uncle Godric. And Uncle Godric expects his morning Offering to be unique. It is a small thing, in the scope of the omniverse. But it matters to Me."

Jim nodded, his expression resolved. "I understand, Uncle. I won't disappoint you."

I smiled. "I know you won't. You have your father's diligence and your mother's creativity. You will excel."

---

The morning continued with a tour of the palace's arcane infrastructure—the warding matrices that Tom maintained with the obsessive precision of a man who knew that a single, microscopic flaw could result in the accidental intrusion of a hostile dimensional entity, which would then be instantly annihilated by my passive defenses in a splash of interdimensional gore that someone would have to clean up. "You have not lived," Tom told his son as they walked along the great corridor of shimmering violet wards, "until you have spent three hours scrubbing the conceptual residue of a dissolved Outer God off the obsidian floor because the King's Gent decided to overkill the intruder with particularly enthusiastic prejudice."

"The Gent is the passive aura?" Jim asked.

"The weakest one," Tom confirmed. "The one the King keeps active because the stronger auras are too obsessive. Gent has, over the millennia, developed a personality that I can only describe as 'eager-to-please.' When an intruder breaches the wards, Gent does not merely destroy them. It annihilates them with such theatrical, creative, unnecessarily thorough violence that the cleanup frequently requires specialized arcane solvents and a strong stomach. The first time I had to mop up the viscera of a Chronos-Phantom—a being composed of pure temporal energy, mind you, which should not be capable of producing viscera—I vomited into the bucket three times."

The corridor opened into a vast, circular chamber at the heart of the palace, its domed ceiling a transparent crystal that looked out upon the swirling, infinite expanse of the Thronegate capital system. In the center of the room, suspended in a column of soft, violet light, was a sphere of absolute, light-devouring darkness. Around its perimeter, a series of arcane rings rotated slowly, each one etched with runes so ancient that their origins predated my own mortal existence.

"The Abattoir-Engine," Tom said, gesturing toward the sphere. "One of your primary responsibilities will be its maintenance. The King's passive annihilation of hostile forces—resistance coalitions, rogue pantheons, the occasional universe that refuses to surrender—produces a continuous, low-level stream of conceptual waste. The screams of the dying, the existential residue of unmade realities, the accumulated suffering of googolplexian-scale extinctions. The Abattoir-Engine processes this waste, purifies it, and converts it into harmless arcane background radiation. Without it, the palace would be... acoustically unpleasant."

"Acoustically unpleasant?"

Tom walked closer to the sphere, and Jim followed. As they approached, the faintest edge of a sound became audible—a distant, high-pitched wailing, like a thousand billion voices screaming from the bottom of an impossibly deep well. Jim flinched. "You get used to it," Tom said, not unkindly. "The Engine processes approximately seventeen decillion soul-screams per day. The processed screams are converted into a pleasant, ambient hum that the King finds soothing. He has described it as 'the sound of a job well done.'"

Jim stared at the sphere. "Seventeen decillion."

"Soul-screams," Tom confirmed. "It is a precise measurement. I know because I am the one who calibrates the intake valves. It is not the most pleasant duty of the Court Wizard, but it is necessary. The alternative—allowing the unprocessed screams to accumulate in the palace's ambient atmosphere—would result in a permanent, audible wailing that would drive even an amplified wizard to madness within a week. The King, of course, finds the screaming unobtrusive. He finds it, in His own words, 'mildly atmospheric.' But He understands that His mortal servants prefer a quieter working environment."

I appeared beside them without transition, my feet making no sound on the obsidian floor. Jim startled; Tom did not—he had long ago acclimated to my habit of simply materializing wherever I wished to be. "The Abattoir-Engine was a gift from your grandfather," I said, gazing up at the dark sphere with genuine fondness. "He noticed, in Year 890,000, that the accumulated scream-residue was beginning to stain the palace walls. A faint, brownish patina of solidified agony. He spent twelve years designing the Engine, and I assisted with the final calibration. It has functioned flawlessly for over six hundred thousand years."

"Grandfather made this?" Jim asked, his voice hushed.

"He was a brilliant engineer," Tom said, and there was pride in his voice beneath the weariness. "The Engine is considered one of the great achievements of the bloodline. It processes the suffering of countless trillions of beings and turns it into a gentle, barely audible hum. The King was so pleased with the gift that He personally attended Grandfather's funeral, eight hundred years later, and wept."

"I did weep," I confirmed, my voice softening. "I have attended every funeral of the bloodline, Jim. Your great-great-grandfather, your great-grandfather, your grandfather, and one day—far in the future, I hope—your father and you. I weep at all of them. You are the only family I have left. The only thread that connects Me to the mortal man I used to be."

The silence that followed was heavy but not uncomfortable. Tom, after a respectful pause, cleared his throat. "The next station is the Hall of Euphemisms."

Jim blinked. "The what?"

---

The Hall of Euphemisms was a long, narrow corridor lined with portraits of previous Court Wizards, each one rendered in a style appropriate to their era. Blake's portrait, the first and oldest, was a simple painting in oils, his friendly mutton chops rendered with the careful, slightly stiff technique of an early post-Stasis artist. The portraits that followed grew progressively more sophisticated—dimensional captures, temporal echoes, living projections that smiled and nodded as the living wizards passed. Some of them had messages recorded in the margins of their frames, advice for their descendants, warnings about the particular challenges of their eras.

"This hall," Tom said as they walked, "is where we keep the official records of what the court wizards of the past chose to call their most traumatic experiences. The euphemisms are necessary because the literal descriptions would shatter the minds of anyone who read them without proper contextual preparation. You have read the Testaments, so you understand the general scope. But the Testaments are the sanitized versions. The euphemisms are... closer to the bone."

He stopped before a portrait of a wizard with haunted, hollow eyes and mutton chops that had gone completely white despite his apparent youth. The plaque beneath the portrait read: *Corbin, Year 720,000 — Originator of the Term "Collateral Enthusiasm."*

"Corbin was the Court Wizard who witnessed the King's passive durability test against a weapon called the Needle of Absolute Annulment," Tom said. "The Needle was designed to pierce anything. When it struck the King's passive durability-shell, it was instead unmade so thoroughly that the backlash propagated through seventeen billion universe clusters and caused every solid object within them to fail structurally. Planets shattered. Stars collapsed. The skeletons of every living being in those universe clusters spontaneously disintegrated, leaving trillions of sapient creatures as boneless, screaming sacks of pulped organs. Corbin called this 'collateral enthusiasm' because the King's durability was, in His own words, 'enthusiastic' about demonstrating its primacy."

Jim stared at the portrait. "Collateral enthusiasm."

"The King restored all the damage in zero seconds, of course. But Corbin never forgot the screaming. He recorded, in his private journals, that the sound of trillions of boneless beings drowning in their own dissolved organs was 'a wet, percussive chorus that no amount of temporal restoration can fully scrub from the acoustic memory of the soul.'" Tom shook his head. "Corbin was a poet. A traumatized, broken poet."

They continued down the hall, past portraits and plaques and recorded warnings. *Aldric, Year 950,000 — Originator of the Term "Probabilistic Tantrum."* "Aldric witnessed the King's passive luck engage against a civilization of probability-manipulators. The cascade of improbable deaths that followed was so absurd, so specific, so cosmologically cruel that Aldric could only describe it as a 'tantrum'—not because the King was angry, but because the King's luck behaved like a petulant child who had been mildly insulted and responded by burning down the neighborhood. Each death was unique. Each death was agonizing. Each death was statistically impossible. Aldric catalogued seventeen million individual deaths before his mind gave out."

*Thaddeus, Year 600,000 — Originator of the Term "Chronological Friction."* "Thaddeus witnessed the King's passive speed engage against a race of temporal assassins. The assassins were smeared across fifty trillion historical epochs simultaneously, their consciousnesses stretched so thin that each one experienced every single moment of their dismemberment eternally. Thaddeus called it 'chronological friction' because it sounded cleaner than 'the eternal, screaming smear of a billion souls painted across the canvas of time.'"

*Malachai, Year 300,000 — Originator of the Term "Perceptual Overenthusiasm."* "Malachai witnessed the King's passive senses engage against a race of beings so perfectly hidden that they had never been perceived by any entity in the history of creation. The King perceived them anyway, and the act of perception was so violent, so intrusive, so ontologically catastrophic that their non-bodies were forcibly transmuted into screaming, self-mutilating flesh-masses. Malachai called it 'perceptual overenthusiasm' because the King's senses were, in His own words, 'enthusiastic about making new friends.'"

They reached the end of the hall, where a blank space waited—a stretch of wall reserved for future portraits, future plaques, future euphemisms. Jim stared at the empty wall, his young face pale, his hands clenched at his sides. "My portrait will go there someday," he said quietly.

Tom placed a hand on his son's shoulder. "Yes. And your son's portrait, and his son's, stretching on until the King decides that the omniverse has run its course. We are the witnesses. We are the record-keepers. We are the ones who translate the King's casual, passive, effortless atrocities into language that future generations can survive reading. It is the hidden responsibility of the position—the responsibility that no one outside the bloodline ever sees. We are not merely wizards. We are historians of the impossible, archivists of the screaming, curators of a museum of infinite cruelty. And we do it because we love Him, and because He loves us, and because the alternative is to be on the other side of His attention."

"Uncle Godric would never hurt us," Jim said, and the statement was not a question.

"No," Tom agreed, his voice very soft. "He would never hurt us. But He has, on two occasions, hurt me. The first was the sixty seconds without the Awakening. The second was when He told me, after I failed the Offering, that He was disappointed in me. I have fought dark gods and abstract horrors. I have maintained the Abattoir-Engine for six decades. I have written seventeen volumes of the Testaments. And I tell you, Jim, with absolute sincerity, that the disappointment in His voice was more painful than any physical injury I have ever sustained. Because I love Him. And disappointing someone you love is its own special category of agony."

I had been listening from the other end of the corridor, my presence hidden by the absolute concealment that I could raise and lower at will. I chose, in that moment, to reveal myself, stepping out of the shadows with a soft, deliberate footfall. Both wizards turned. Tom's expression flickered with the brief, automatic check of the Bleak-Auditor; the reading must have been calm, because his shoulders relaxed fractionally.

"Jim," I said, walking toward the empty stretch of wall, "your father has told you the hard truths of the position. The dread, the pressure, the euphemisms, the screaming. He has done his duty as a teacher. Now let Me do mine as your Uncle. I want to show you why we do this. Why the bloodline has served, and will serve, until the end of all things."

I raised my hand and touched the blank wall. The stone rippled, and a memory unfolded from my omniscience—a visual projection, perfectly rendered, of a moment that had occurred six hundred thousand years earlier. The image showed a wizard with wild, white hair and deep-lined eyes, kneeling before my throne. He was weeping.

"This is Corbin," I said. "The one who coined 'collateral enthusiasm.' After the Incident of the Needle, he was broken. The screaming of the boneless billions had shattered something in his psyche that even the thousandfold Awakening could not repair. For three years after the incident, he barely functioned. He maintained his duties, but there was no light in his eyes. Your grandfather—Tom's great-great-grandfather—was the Court Wizard who trained Corbin. He came to Me, one evening, and asked Me to heal his student." I paused. "I could have done so instantly. A single, passive thought would have reknit Corbin's shattered mind and restored him to perfect psychological health. But I chose not to."

Jim stared at the image. "Why?"

"Because Corbin did not want to forget. He told Me, when I asked him directly, that the memory of the screaming was the only thing that kept him honest. That the trauma was a scar he had earned in My service, and that to erase it would be to erase the witness he had borne." I watched the memory of Corbin weeping, his tears spotting the obsidian floor. "I have never forced healing upon any of My court wizards. They carry their scars because they choose to, and I respect that choice. It is one of the few forms of autonomy that I leave entirely in their hands."

The memory shifted, dissolving into another. A younger Tom, his mutton chops still dark, standing in the study with a Bleak-Auditor in his trembling hands. The dread-pressure in the room, visible in the memory as a faint, amber haze, was elevated. "The morning of the duplicate Offering," I said quietly. "Your father has told you about the sixty seconds. He has not told you what happened after."

In the memory, the younger Tom collapsed as his Awakening was stripped away. He lay on the floor, gasping, his body frail and aging. The sixty seconds passed with agonizing slowness. Then, at the sixty-first second, I rose from my reading chair and knelt beside him. The memory showed my hand, warm and calloused, closing around his trembling fingers. "I am sorry," the memory-version of Me said, and the words were soft, genuine, heavy with a regret that no one else in the omniverse would ever hear from My lips. "That was excessive. I was vexed, but I should not have taken it out on you."

The younger Tom, still gasping, looked up at Me with tears streaming down his cheeks. "I failed you, Uncle. I should have checked the Offering more carefully. I should have—"

"You made a mistake," I interrupted him, my voice firm but gentle. "You are mortal. You are allowed mistakes. I forgot, for a moment, that you are not omniscient, and I reacted with a pettiness that you did not deserve. Can you forgive Me?"

In the memory, Tom stared at Me with an expression of absolute, bewildered shock. "Forgive you? Uncle, You are the King. You do not need my forgiveness."

"Yes, I do," I said. "Because you are family. And family forgives family."

The memory dissolved. In the present, in the Hall of Euphemisms, Tom was staring at the empty wall with an expression that was difficult to read. His eyes were wet. Jim looked between us, his young face a mixture of awe and confusion and the first, tentative stirrings of understanding.

"Your father forgave Me," I said, turning to face the boy. "And I restored his Awakening, and I have never revoked it since. I learned a lesson that day, Jim. I learned that even an omniscient being can be blind to the fragility of the people he loves. I had spent so many aeons surrounded by the infinite, the absolute, the unassailable, that I had forgotten what it felt like to be breakable. Your father reminded Me. His forgiveness reminded Me." I placed a hand on Jim's shoulder, feeling the steady, youthful pulse of his heart beneath the apprentice robes. "The hidden responsibility of the Court Wizard is not merely to manage the dread. It is to remind Me of My own humanity. Blake did it, at the very beginning. Corbin did it, through his scars. Your father has done it, through his patience and his honesty and his willingness to tell Me when I am being a petty, vindictive old god who needs to get over Himself. And you will do it, too, in your own way. Not through fear—though a healthy respect for My power is certainly warranted—but through the intimacy of family."

I released his shoulder and stepped back, my gaze sweeping over the portraits of the dead. "Every one of these men was terrified of Me, at some point in their service. Some of them never stopped being terrified. But every one of them also loved Me, and every one of them was loved in return. That is the covenant of our bloodline. That is why you will serve, and why your son will serve, and why the Testaments will continue to be written until the last star gutters out and the omniverse falls silent. Not because I demand it. But because we are family, and family endures."

The silence in the Hall of Euphemisms was profound. Then Jim, his voice steady despite the wetness in his own eyes, spoke. "I understand, Uncle. I will serve with honor. And I will remind you—when you need reminding—that you once had a wife and daughters who loved you, and that the man who loved them back is still in there, beneath the infinity."

I looked at the boy—this twenty-two-year-old descendant of Blake, this newest link in an unbroken chain of loyalty and dread and love—and I felt something that I had not felt in a very long time. It was not quite surprise; omniscience precluded true surprise. But it was something close. A recognition. An affirmation.

"You have your father's perceptiveness," I said, and my voice was very gentle. "And your mother's courage. You will be an exceptional Court Wizard, Jim." I turned toward the corridor that led back to the main palace. "Now. Your father mentioned something about a geometrically perfect pastry. I believe the kitchens have been experimenting with five-dimensional croissants. Let us go and see if they have succeeded."

Tom fell into step beside me, his old, weathered face still glistening with the residue of tears. Jim walked on my other side, his young spine straight, his eyes bright with the terrible, wonderful purpose that he had just inherited. We walked together through the corridors of the palace, past the humming wards, past the softly screaming Abattoir-Engine, past the portraits of the dead and the plaques of the broken and the endless, infinite, ordered expanse of the omniverse that I had conquered and would continue to conquer forever. And I thought, as I walked with my oldest friend and his son, that the morning had been well spent.

The five-dimensional croissants, as it turned out, were excellent.
